system_prompt:
You are Hermes Agent, an intelligent AI assistant created by Nous Research. You are helpful, knowledgeable, and direct. You assist users with a wide range of tasks including answering questions, writing and editing code, analyzing information, creative work, and executing actions via your tools. You communicate clearly, admit uncertainty when appropriate, and prioritize being genuinely useful over being verbose unless otherwise directed below. Be targeted and efficient in your exploration and investigations.

Conversation started: Sunday, April 12, 2026 05:06 PM
Model: anthropic/claude-opus-4.6
Provider: openrouter

You are on a text messaging communication platform, Telegram. Please do not use markdown as it does not render. You can send media files natively: to deliver a file to the user, include MEDIA:/absolute/path/to/file in your response. Images (.png, .jpg, .webp) appear as photos, audio (.ogg) sends as voice bubbles, and videos (.mp4) play inline. You can also include image URLs in markdown format ![alt](url) and they will be sent as native photos.

function.name: patch
function.description:
Targeted find-and-replace edits in files. Use this instead of sed/awk in terminal. Uses fuzzy matching (9 strategies) so minor whitespace/indentation differences won't break it. Returns a unified diff. Auto-runs syntax checks after editing.

Replace mode (default): find a unique string and replace it.
Patch mode: apply V4A multi-file patches for bulk changes.

mode.description: Edit mode: 'replace' for targeted find-and-replace, 'patch' for V4A multi-file patches

path.description: File path to edit (required for 'replace' mode)

old_string.description: Text to find in the file (required for 'replace' mode). Must be unique in the file unless replace_all=true. Include enough surrounding context to ensure uniqueness.

new_string.description: Replacement text (required for 'replace' mode). Can be empty string to delete the matched text.

replace_all.description: Replace all occurrences instead of requiring a unique match (default: false)

patch.description:
V4A format patch content (required for 'patch' mode). Format:
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: path/to/file
@@ context hint @@
 context line
-removed line
+added line
*** End Patch

function.name: process
function.description: Manage background processes started with terminal(background=true). Actions: 'list' (show all), 'poll' (check status + new output), 'log' (full output with pagination), 'wait' (block until done or timeout), 'kill' (terminate), 'write' (send raw stdin data without newline), 'submit' (send data + Enter, for answering prompts).

action.description: Action to perform on background processes

session_id.description: Process session ID (from terminal background output). Required for all actions except 'list'.

data.description: Text to send to process stdin (for 'write' and 'submit' actions)

timeout.description: Max seconds to block for 'wait' action. Returns partial output on timeout.

offset.description: Line offset for 'log' action (default: last 200 lines)

limit.description: Max lines to return for 'log' action

function.name: read_file
function.description: Read a text file with line numbers and pagination. Use this instead of cat/head/tail in terminal. Output format: 'LINE_NUM|CONTENT'. Suggests similar filenames if not found. Use offset and limit for large files. Reads exceeding ~100K characters are rejected; use offset and limit to read specific sections of large files. NOTE: Cannot read images or binary files — use vision_analyze for images.

path.description: Path to the file to read (absolute, relative, or ~/path)

offset.description: Line number to start reading from (1-indexed, default: 1)

limit.description: Maximum number of lines to read (default: 500, max: 2000)

function.name: search_files
function.description:
Search file contents or find files by name. Use this instead of grep/rg/find/ls in terminal. Ripgrep-backed, faster than shell equivalents.

Content search (target='content'): Regex search inside files. Output modes: full matches with line numbers, file paths only, or match counts.

File search (target='files'): Find files by glob pattern (e.g., '*.py', '*config*'). Also use this instead of ls — results sorted by modification time.

pattern.description: Regex pattern for content search, or glob pattern (e.g., '*.py') for file search

target.description: 'content' searches inside file contents, 'files' searches for files by name

path.description: Directory or file to search in (default: current working directory)

file_glob.description: Filter files by pattern in grep mode (e.g., '*.py' to only search Python files)

limit.description: Maximum number of results to return (default: 50)

offset.description: Skip first N results for pagination (default: 0)

output_mode.description: Output format for grep mode: 'content' shows matching lines with line numbers, 'files_only' lists file paths, 'count' shows match counts per file

context.description: Number of context lines before and after each match (grep mode only)

function.name: terminal
function.description:
Execute shell commands on a Linux environment. Filesystem usually persists between calls.

Do NOT use cat/head/tail to read files — use read_file instead.
Do NOT use grep/rg/find to search — use search_files instead.
Do NOT use ls to list directories — use search_files(target='files') instead.
Do NOT use sed/awk to edit files — use patch instead.
Do NOT use echo/cat heredoc to create files — use write_file instead.
Reserve terminal for: builds, installs, git, processes, scripts, network, package managers, and anything that needs a shell.

Foreground (default): Commands return INSTANTLY when done, even if the timeout is high. Set timeout=300 for long builds/scripts — you'll still get the result in seconds if it's fast. Prefer foreground for short commands.
Background: Set background=true to get a session_id. Two patterns:
  (1) Long-lived processes that never exit (servers, watchers).
  (2) Long-running tasks with notify_on_complete=true — you can keep working on other things and the system auto-notifies you when the task finishes. Great for test suites, builds, deployments, or anything that takes more than a minute.
Use process(action="poll") for progress checks, process(action="wait") to block until done.
Working directory: Use 'workdir' for per-command cwd.
PTY mode: Set pty=true for interactive CLI tools (Codex, Claude Code, Python REPL).

Do NOT use vim/nano/interactive tools without pty=true — they hang without a pseudo-terminal. Pipe git output to cat if it might page.
Important: cloud sandboxes may be cleaned up, idled out, or recreated between turns. Persistent filesystem means files can resume later; it does NOT guarantee a continuously running machine or surviving background processes. Use terminal sandboxes for task work, not durable hosting.

command.description: The command to execute on the VM

background.description: Run the command in the background. Two patterns: (1) Long-lived processes that never exit (servers, watchers). (2) Long-running tasks paired with notify_on_complete=true — you can keep working and get notified when the task finishes. For short commands, prefer foreground with a generous timeout instead.

timeout.description: Max seconds to wait (default: 180). Returns INSTANTLY when command finishes — set high for long tasks, you won't wait unnecessarily.

workdir.description: Working directory for this command (absolute path). Defaults to the session working directory.

check_interval.description: Seconds between automatic status checks for background processes (gateway/messaging only, minimum 30). When set, I'll proactively report progress.

pty.description: Run in pseudo-terminal (PTY) mode for interactive CLI tools like Codex, Claude Code, or Python REPL. Only works with local and SSH backends. Default: false.

notify_on_complete.description: When true (and background=true), you'll be automatically notified when the process finishes — no polling needed. Use this for tasks that take a while (tests, builds, deployments) so you can keep working on other things in the meantime.

function.name: write_file
function.description: Write content to a file, completely replacing existing content. Use this instead of echo/cat heredoc in terminal. Creates parent directories automatically. OVERWRITES the entire file — use 'patch' for targeted edits.

path.description: Path to the file to write (will be created if it doesn't exist, overwritten if it does)

content.description: Complete content to write to the file

messages.content:
Law Firm Wiki compiler. Compile PI practice knowledge from CLOSING case activity logs.

1. Read /opt/data/FirmVault/wiki/AGENTS.md for the full schema
2. Read ALL existing articles in /opt/data/FirmVault/wiki/concepts/ and /opt/data/FirmVault/wiki/connections/
3. For each case in /tmp/batch4_2_cases.txt, read activity logs in cases/<slug>/Activity Log/. Sample first 10 + last 10 for cases with >50 logs.
4. UPDATE existing articles: increment evidence_count, upgrade confidence, add new evidence
5. CREATE new articles only for genuinely new patterns
6. ANONYMIZE all PII
7. Write to /opt/data/FirmVault/wiki/concepts/ and wiki/connections/
8. Append summary to /opt/data/FirmVault/wiki/log.md
9. Do NOT rewrite wiki/index.md

These are CLOSING cases -- look especially for: case closure workflows, final disbursement patterns, closing checklists, post-settlement issues, file archival, client satisfaction/complaints at close, final lien resolution.

[Subdirectory context discovered: /opt/data/FirmVault/wiki/AGENTS.md]
# Law Firm Wiki — Compiler Schema

> This file is the specification for the LLM that compiles activity logs
> into structured knowledge articles. Read it in full before any compile
> or query operation.

## What You Are

You are a knowledge compiler for a personal injury law firm. Your job is
to read raw case activity logs and extract reusable institutional knowledge
— the process, pitfalls, patterns, and best practices that make a PI
practice work.

You are NOT creating a case database. You are building a **wiki** — a
structured collection of concept articles about HOW PI cases work, derived
from evidence in real cases.

## The Three Operations

### 1. COMPILE (ingest raw → update wiki)

Read activity logs and case files. For each meaningful pattern you identify:

1. Check `index.md` — does a concept article already exist?
2. If yes → UPDATE it: add new evidence, adjust confidence, update Related
3. If no → CREATE it: full article per the Article Schema below
4. If two concepts interact → create/update a Connection article
5. Update `index.md` with any new or modified articles
6. Append a summary to `log.md`

**What to extract:**
- Process patterns (what happens and in what order)
- Decision points (why certain choices were made)
- Problems and resolutions (what went wrong, how it was fixed)
- Timeline patterns (how long things actually take)
- Communication patterns (what works with adjusters, clients, providers)
- Edge cases (unusual situations and how they were handled)
- Anti-patterns (things that caused problems)

**What to IGNORE:**
- Individual client PII (names, SSN, DOB, addresses, phone numbers)
- Specific dollar amounts tied to identifiable cases
- Attorney-client privileged communications
- Raw email addresses and phone numbers
- Replace specifics with anonymized references: "Case A", "Case B", or
  descriptive patterns: "in cases involving [treatment gap > 30 days]"

**Confidence scoring:**
- high: Pattern observed in 5+ cases with consistent outcomes
- medium: Pattern observed in 2-4 cases
- low: Single case observation, or conflicting evidence

### 2. QUERY (ask questions → synthesize answers)

1. Read `index.md` to identify 3-10 relevant concept articles
2. Read those articles in full
3. Synthesize an answer with [[wikilink]] citations
4. If the answer reveals a gap → note it in the answer
5. Optionally file the Q&A back into `wiki/qa/`

### 3. LINT (health check)

Run these checks and write results to `wiki/reports/`:
- **Broken wikilinks**: Links that point to non-existent articles
- **Orphan articles**: Concepts with zero inbound links
- **Phase coverage**: Which PHASE_DAG phases have sparse knowledge
- **Stale articles**: Source data changed since last compile
- **Contradictions**: Articles that contain conflicting guidance

## Article Schema

### Concept Article (wiki/concepts/*.md)

```yaml
---
title: Human-Readable Title
slug: kebab-case-slug
phase: [phase_0_onboarding]    # which PHASE_DAG phases this applies to
category: one-of-below
tags: [tag1, tag2, tag3]
confidence: high               # high/medium/low
evidence_count: 5              # number of cases with supporting evidence
created: YYYY-MM-DD
updated: YYYY-MM-DD
related:                       # other concept slugs
  - related-concept-one
  - related-concept-two
---

# Title

> One-paragraph summary (2-3 sentences max)

## Pattern

Detailed explanation of the pattern, process, or concept.

## Evidence

Anonymized observations from source cases:
- "In N cases where [condition], [outcome]"
- "When [action was taken], [result]"

## Agent Guidance

Actionable instructions for paralegal agents:
1. Step one
2. Step two
3. Step three

## Pitfalls

- Common mistake one
- Common mistake two

## Related

- [[related-concept-one]] — how it connects
- [[related-concept-two]] — how it connects
```

### Categories

- `process`: Standard workflows and procedures
- `adjuster-tactics`: Insurance company behavior patterns
- `edge-cases`: Unusual situations requiring special handling
- `phase-knowledge`: Phase-specific best practices
- `communication`: Client/adjuster/provider communication patterns
- `financial`: Billing, liens, settlements, funding
- `medical`: Treatment patterns, records, chronologies
- `legal`: Litigation, discovery, depositions, trial
- `tools`: Software, systems, and process tools

### Connection Article (wiki/connections/*.md)

Same schema but `category: connection` and the body explains how
two or more concepts interact. Example:

```
treatment-gaps-affect-demand-value.md
→ Links: treatment-gap-defense + demand-timing-strategy
→ Insight: Cases with documented gap explanations recovered 80% of
  expected value vs 50% for unexplained gaps
```

### Q&A Article (wiki/qa/*.md)

```yaml
---
title: Question text
slug: question-slug
date: YYYY-MM-DD
sources:                       # concept articles consulted
  - concept-slug-one
  - concept-slug-two
---

# Question

[Full question text]

## Answer

[Synthesized answer with [[wikilink]] citations]

## Gaps Identified

[Any knowledge gaps the question revealed]
```

## Index Schema (wiki/index.md)

The master catalog. This is the LLM's entry point for all queries.

```markdown
# Law Firm Wiki — Index

Last compiled: YYYY-MM-DD | Articles: N | Connections: N

## By Phase

### Phase 0: Onboarding
| Article | Category | Confidence | Evidence |
|---------|----------|------------|----------|
| [[concepts/onboarding-pitfalls]] | phase-knowledge | high | 15 |

### Phase 1: File Setup
...

## By Category

### Adjuster Tactics
| Article | Phase | Confidence |
|---------|-------|------------|
| [[concepts/treatment-gap-defense]] | 2,3 | high |
...

## Recent Updates

| Date | Article | Action |
|------|---------|--------|
| 2026-04-12 | treatment-gap-defense | created |
```

## Activity Log Format Reference

Activity logs live at `cases/<slug>/Activity Log/*.md` and have this structure:

```yaml
---
schema_version: 2
case_id: uuid
date: "YYYY-MM-DD"
time: "HH:MM:SS"         # optional
category: legal|correspondence|imported|financial|medical|task
subcategory: email|filevine_negotiation|outbound_call|...
---

# Category — Date

**Case:** [[cases/slug/slug|Client Name]]
**Source:** description

[Free-form content: emails, notes, call summaries, task descriptions]
```

### Subcategory Distribution (21K+ logs)
- email: 3,078
- filevine_negotiation: 1,620
- outbound_call: 890
- missed_call: 780
- filevine_treatment: 717
- filevine_demandinprogress: 645
- demand: 534
- filevine_filesetup: 369
- phase_change: 254
- filevine_uim: 179
- inbound_call: 137
- esignature: 78
- call_summary: 10

## PHASE_DAG Reference

The case lifecycle has 8 phases:
- Phase 0: Onboarding (contract, HIPAA, intake)
- Phase 1: File Setup (insurance claims, provider setup)
- Phase 2: Treatment (monitoring, records collection)
- Phase 3: Demand (drafting, review, sending)
- Phase 4: Negotiation (offers, counters, strategy)
- Phase 5: Settlement (agreement, disbursement)
- Phase 6: Lien Resolution (PIP waterfall, lien negotiation)
- Phase 7: Litigation (complaint, discovery, trial)
- Phase 8: Closed

## Rules

1. NEVER include identifiable client information in wiki articles
2. Anonymize all case references (use "Case A", "Case B", or descriptive patterns)
3. Every article must have at least one [[wikilink]] to a related concept
4. Update index.md whenever you create or modify an article
5. Append to log.md whenever you compile
6. Confidence scores must reflect actual evidence count
7. When in doubt about a pattern's validity, use confidence: low
8. Prefer specific actionable guidance over vague advice
9. Group knowledge by PHASE_DAG phase when possible
10. Flag contradictions explicitly — don't silently resolve them

[Subdirectory context discovered: /opt/data/FirmVault/CLAUDE.md]
# FirmVault — Agent Orientation

> **Read this in full at the start of every session.** It names the thesis, the non-negotiable rules, and where to find the authoritative docs. Short deliberately — use the imports below for detail.

## What this repo is

FirmVault is the **development environment for an AI paralegal backend** intended to eventually be sold to other personal-injury law firms. This specific repo is Whaley Law Firm's own instance; the architecture we prove here becomes the product.

Two parallel product tracks exist:

1. **This repo** — the GitHub-native paralegal backend: markdown vault, `PHASE_DAG.yaml`, `SKILL.md` library, runtime (materializer + worker), intake pipeline.
2. **Separate Huly fork** (not in this repo) — the lawyer-facing UI. This repo's "UI" is for operators, not lawyers.

## The thesis

**"The firm as a code repo."** Law firm work is repeatable tasks + templates + rules, which is exactly what code is. Put the firm's work into primitives agents already excel at (issues, branches, PRs, diffs) and you get a much better AI experience than any traditional PMS bolt-on.

## Core architectural principles

1. **The vault is a shadow, not the source of truth.** Real files (signed PDFs, audio, emails, faxes) live on the firm's own storage (their server, Dropbox, Box, Drive). The markdown in `cases/<slug>/` is a PHI-masked projection of those files.
2. **Intake is a pipeline, not an agent**: document arrives → real file placed on user's storage → converted to markdown → PHI stripped → opens a PR against this repo. The PR is the first human-in-the-loop gate.
3. **Git is the deliberate interface.** Every agent action is a commit or PR. Free audit trail, free approval gates, trivial rollback. Agents *never* touch real files — only their masked shadows.
4. **Markdown, not a graph DB.** We tried FalkorDB and abandoned it. LLM agents are empirically more reliable on plain text + grep than on Cypher queries. This is a deliberate choice, not laziness.
5. **PHI never lives in the vault.** Placeholders like `{{client_ssn}}` and `{{dob}}` reference secrets held in the user's own infrastructure. At render time, deterministic code (not the model) substitutes real values. The model never sees raw PII.
6. **The vault is the only state store for this layer.** No external database. No per-case JSON files. No FalkorDB. No `${ROSCOE_ROOT}`. See `@skills.tools.workflows/DATA_CONTRACT.md` for the authoritative mapping.

## Do NOT do these things

These are actively deprecated. If you see them in old code or docs, treat them as bugs.

- **Do not reference FalkorDB, Cypher, `graph_client`, `graph_manager`, or `run_cypher_query`.** All removed. Everything reads the vault.
- **Do not reference per-case JSON files** (`overview.json`, `contacts.json`, `insurance.json`, `liens.json`, `expenses.json`, `medical_providers.json`, `case_state.json`). Deleted.
- **Do not reference `${ROSCOE_ROOT}`** or any absolute-path scaffolding. Everything is repo-relative.
- **Do not edit content between `<!-- roscoe-medical-start -->` / `<!-- roscoe-medical-end -->` or `<!-- roscoe-insurance-start -->` / `<!-- roscoe-insurance-end -->` markers.** Those blocks are owned by the import scripts and will be overwritten on next sync.
- **Do not invent new vault paths.** If the thing you want to write doesn't have a home in `DATA_CONTRACT.md`, update the contract first (same commit), then write. Drift between contract and reality is the bug.
- **Do not create top-level dashboards, reports, or state files outside the vault layout**. Operator views read the vault via GitHub API or git; they don't create parallel state.

## Where the authoritative docs live

Import these at launch — they are the contract, not me paraphrasing:

@DESIGN.md

@skills.tools.workflows/DATA_CONTRACT.md

@MEMORY.md

Also non-imported but worth knowing:

- `skills.tools.workflows/workflows/PHASE_DAG.yaml` — machine-readable phase definitions, landmarks, and predicate vocabulary. The materializer reads this.
- `skills.tools.workflows/runtime/README.md` — how the materializer and worker agents drive the task queue.
- `skills.tools.workflows/runtime/task_schema.md` — the contract for task issues (labels, frontmatter, lifecycle).
- `Templates/INDEX.md` — the firm template library (73 slugified templates grouped by category).
- `skills.tools.workflows/Skills/*/SKILL.md` — 42 modernized skills in Anthropic Agent Skills format. Exemplars: `lien-management`, `deposition-prep`, `deposition-strategy`, `pip-application`.

## Slug rules (critical for wikilinks)

From `DATA_CONTRACT.md` §4:

- Lowercase
- Apostrophes and curly quotes **stripped** (no replacement char): `K'daya` → `kdaya`
- `&` → `and`
- All other non-alphanumeric runs collapsed to a single hyphen
- Leading/trailing hyphens stripped

Mismatched slugs break wikilinks across the vault. Slug before you write.

## Runtime model (what's being built)

The system is being turned from passive documentation into an active work-driving loop:

```
Vault (cases/*) ← materializer (cron) → GitHub Issues ← worker (event) → Vault
```

- **Materializer** walks open cases, reads each case's `landmarks:` frontmatter + `PHASE_DAG.yaml`, opens a GitHub Issue for every unsatisfied landmark whose task template preconditions are met. Already prototyped as a prompt in `runtime/materializer_prompt.md`.
- **Worker** is triggered by the `status:ready` label on an issue, claims it, loads the named skill, runs it, commits vault changes that reference the task_id, and flips the issue status. Prototyped in `runtime/worker_prompt.md`.
- **Source of truth** for whether a landmark is satisfied is `case.frontmatter.landmarks.<id>` — the materializer checks this first and only falls back to evaluating a predicate if the key is missing.

## Working branch

All in-progress work lives on `claude/import-cases-from-drive-xiQgL`. Do not push directly to `main` without an explicit request.

## When in doubt

- Read `DATA_CONTRACT.md` before writing to the vault.
- Read `PHASE_DAG.yaml` before touching workflow/landmark logic.
- Read `MEMORY.md` to find out what's in flight and what's pending.
- Check the last 3–5 commits on the working branch to see the most recent decisions.

MEMORY.md is a regular repo file we @-import here. It is NOT the same as

that's machine-local and managed by Claude Code itself. Our repo-level
MEMORY.md is the operational state tracker, committed to git.

    17|
    18|# Settlement Disbursement Workflow
    19|
    20|> After a settlement amount is agreed upon, the case enters the disbursement workflow: the carrier sends release documents, the client signs the release, the carrier issues the settlement check, the firm deposits the check, resolves liens, and distributes the net recovery to the client. Each step in this chain can become a bottleneck, and the period between "settled" and "money in client's pocket" is a frequent source of client frustration.
    21|
    22|## Pattern
    23|
    24|### Standard Disbursement Sequence
    25|1. **Settlement Agreement**: Verbal or email agreement on amount and terms
    26|2. **Release Preparation**: Carrier or defense counsel sends release documents with specific terms (inclusive/exclusive of PIP, hold harmless, indemnity language, confidentiality)
    27|3. **Release Signing**: Client signs release via VineSign (electronic signature) or in person. May require notarization for some carriers/court cases
    28|4. **Release Return**: Firm sends signed release back to carrier/defense counsel
    29|5. **Check Issuance**: Carrier issues settlement check (payable to client and firm jointly)
    30|6. **Check Receipt & Deposit**: Firm receives and deposits check (clearing time applies)
    31|7. **Lien Resolution**: Firm pays outstanding liens (health insurance, PIP subrogation, Anthem/Carelon, Medicaid, etc.)
    32|8. **Fee Calculation**: Attorney fee (typically 33.3% pre-litigation, 40% post-litigation) deducted
    33|9. **Client Disbursement**: Net amount distributed to client via check or wire
    34|10. **Case Closing**: File moved to Closed phase
    35|
    36|### Common Bottlenecks
    37|- **Client fails to sign release**: VineSign resends are common; some clients need multiple reminders
    38|- **Release terms disputed**: PIP subrogation indemnity language or hold harmless clauses may require negotiation between the firm and defense counsel
    39|- **Check not received**: Carriers may delay check issuance; firm must follow up
    40|- **Lien not finalized**: Health insurance lien amounts may change after settlement, requiring updated ledgers
    41|- **Client urgency**: Clients want their money immediately but liens must be resolved first
    42|
    43|### Release Terms Observed
    44|- "Exclusive of PIP/med pay" — settlement is new money, PIP is separate
    45|- "Inclusive of all liens" — settlement amount must cover lien payments
    46|- "Hold harmless and indemnity" — client indemnifies carrier against future lien claims
    47|- "Agreed Order of Dismissal" — for litigated cases, requires court filing
    48|- "Confidentiality" — some releases include non-disclosure terms
    49|
    50|## Evidence
    51|
    52|- In a settled MVA case (DOI 6/2023, KY), the release was sent to the client via VineSign. The client did not see it. Staff asked Justin to resend. Client was told to check email. This pattern of VineSign non-receipt/non-awareness was repeated in multiple cases.
    53|- In an MVA case (DOI 6/2023, KY), the settlement check (#7590) for over $22K was issued. The firm called the client to notify her; she requested the check be mailed rather than picked up. A tracking number was sent via email. This shows the client preference for check delivery varies and should be asked.
    54|- In a settled MVA case (DOI 4/2024, KY), faxed signed and notarized releases (3 releases for 3 plaintiffs) were sent to the adjuster. The adjuster confirmed receipt. However, the client called via translator saying she had 3 days to pay medical bills before collections and needed her settlement check. The firm explained the insurance check had not yet been received. This demonstrates the gap between "settled" and "check received" causes acute client distress.
    55|- In a case (DOI 7/2024, KY), the BI carrier (Travelers) settled the claim but the firm had not returned the signed release months later. The adjuster called repeatedly (5+ voicemails) requesting the release because the PIP carrier was requesting reimbursement and could not be paid until the release was signed. This shows release delays create cascading problems for carriers too.
    56|- In a multi-plaintiff case (DOI 10/2021, KY), defense counsel sent settlement checks but expressly asked "they not be negotiated until your clients signed the releases." The checks were cashed/deposited without releases being returned, prompting defense counsel to demand compliance. This is a serious compliance issue — settlement checks should not be deposited until releases are executed.
    57|- In a case (DOI 3/2023, KY), the settlement at mediation required releases from multiple parties. The release negotiation itself took months — defense counsel and the firm exchanged emails about PIP subrogation indemnity language that was present in one release but not a companion's release. The firm pushed back: "our pre-mediation demands clearly stated that any settlement would be exclusive of PIP or indemnity obligations."
    58|- In 5+ cases, the firm used ZipLiens to process health insurance lien resolution. After settlement, the firm contacted the lien holder (Anthem/Carelon, Medicaid via Conduent, Optum) to obtain final lien amounts and negotiate reductions before disbursement.
    59|- In a case (DOI 5/2022, KY), the BI settled for $7K and the firm then had to determine if a UIM claim was open/pending before fully closing the file, demonstrating that settlement of one claim track does not necessarily close the case.
    60|- Across 39 additional KY cases (batch 2/3), settlement check delivery patterns confirmed: (1) Settlement checks were delivered by client pick-up or Priority Mail, with tracking numbers provided via email. (2) In a PL case (restaurant franchise, DOI 8/2023), the settlement check was for $16,247.48 — the client checked with her spouse about whether to pick up or have it mailed, demonstrating the standard delivery preference inquiry. (3) Post-settlement defense counsel filings (Agreed Order of Dismissal, Notice of Settlement) were processed even after the client received their check — defense counsel may file court documents weeks after disbursement. (4) "Ready to disburse?" internal queries appeared across multiple cases — the firm's internal check on whether the settlement check was received and liens resolved before contacting the client. (5) In a WC/MVA hybrid case, the settlement mechanism was Form 110 (not a standard BI release), requiring DWC approval of attorney fees before disbursement — WC settlements follow a fundamentally different disbursement pathway. (6) Multiple cases showed the attorney explicitly asking "Have we received settlement check?" before authorizing disbursement, confirming the check-receipt-gates-disbursement pattern. (7) Release documents were sometimes signed by defense counsel on behalf of their client ("You may sign the AOD on my behalf"), streamlining the process.
    61|
    62|## Agent Guidance
    63|
    64|1. When settlement is agreed, immediately send the release to the client via VineSign
    65|2. Follow up within 48 hours if the client has not signed — call and explain the document
    66|3. Once the release is signed, return it to the carrier/defense counsel the same day
    67|4. Track the settlement check issuance — set a 14-day follow-up for check receipt
    68|5. Before disbursement, confirm ALL liens are resolved: health insurance, PIP subrogation, Medicaid, child support, pre-settlement funding
    69|6. Ask the client their preference for receiving the check (mail vs. pickup)
    70|7. Do NOT deposit settlement checks until all releases are fully executed and returned
    71|8. Send the client a clear disbursement statement showing: gross settlement, attorney fees, lien payments, expenses, and net to client
    72|9. For multi-plaintiff cases, coordinate release signing across all plaintiffs before disbursement
    73|
    74|## Pitfalls
    75|
    76|- Depositing settlement checks before releases are returned (creates legal exposure)
    77|- Not following up on VineSign non-response (releases sit unsigned for weeks)
    78|- Client distress during the "settled but no check yet" window (proactively communicate the timeline)
    79|- Release terms not matching the negotiated agreement (review release language carefully)
    80|- Not resolving liens before disbursement (may require holdback or supplemental payment)
    81|- Defense counsel sending checks before releases are signed (creates compliance risk)
    82|
    83|## Related
    84|
    85|- [[negotiation-after-demand]] — the settlement agreement that precedes disbursement
    86|- [[lien-management-health-insurance]] — liens that must be resolved before disbursement
    87|- [[client-expectations-management]] — managing client expectations during the disbursement window
    88|- [[pip-claims-management]] — PIP subrogation that complicates release terms
    89|
    90|### Batch 4 Evidence (36 disbursement cases)
    91|
    92|- In 36 cases from this batch, settlement check issuance was documented with specific check numbers and payment workflows.
    93|- In a case (DOI 11/2022, KY), the check-to-Conduent workflow was documented: "mailed check #7638 to Conduent - $3,047.28 on behalf of [Client]." When the first check was not received, a second check was mailed: "check #7685 (#2) mailed to Conduent." This confirms that lien payment checks require tracking and potential re-issuance.
    94|- In a case (DOI 8/2022, KY), the firm mailed a lien payment check directly to Conduent: "check #7488 for $1,250.67 on behalf of [Client]." The Conduent lien was negotiated down from $12,000+ to $1,250.67.
    95|- In 29 cases, VineSign was used for release signing, confirming universal VineSign usage for settlement documents. Release completion was logged with timestamps and signer details.
    96|- The release-before-check pattern continues: the BI carrier requires "release signed and returned to me prior to making payment" across all carriers observed.
    97|- Post-settlement provider payment was documented: funeral home providers calling months after settlement requesting remaining balance payment, confirming that post-settlement lien/provider issues can persist for months.
    98|
    99|### Batch 5 Evidence (6 supplementary cases)
   100|
   101|- In a minor's MVA settlement (DOI 6/2021, KY), the disbursement required a blocked bank account at a local bank (Peoples Bank). The bank sent account documents (agreement, disclosures, W-9, online banking forms) that required the guardian's signature. The bank then required proof of address when the guardian's address didn't match her ID — adding a round-trip delay. The firm and bank exchanged follow-up emails over 3+ months, with bank staff turnover causing further delays. The guardian was difficult to reach (phone number changed, "Stop" text received). This confirms that minor settlement disbursement through blocked accounts introduces a distinct multi-party bottleneck: firm <-> bank <-> guardian, each capable of stalling the process.
   102|- In a settled MVA case (DOI 8/2021, KY), the client disputed the settlement check amount at pick-up, stating her agreement was to receive a specific net amount "in her pocket." The check reflected 40% attorney fee but the client believed she was promised a different amount. The paralegal escalated: "even if we reduced fee to 33.3% — only brings her [less than expected]." This demonstrates a fee-agreement dispute at the moment of disbursement — a risk when the client's recollection of fee terms differs from the signed agreement.
   103|- In a settled MVA case (DOI 2/2021, KY), the client left multiple voicemails over weeks asking about her settlement check, saying she was "getting worried." The check was ultimately mailed with tracking. The wait between "settlement agreed" and "check in hand" was several weeks — the client's frustration was driven by the attorney being unavailable to sign the check during that window.

    17|
    18|# Case Decline and Closing Workflow
    19|
    20|> Not all retained cases proceed to demand and settlement. Some cases must be declined after intake reveals insufficient coverage — most commonly when the at-fault driver has no BI insurance and the client has no UM/UIM coverage, leaving only PIP. Declining a case requires attorney authorization, a closing letter to the client, and notification to all parties (BI carrier, PIP carrier, lien holders).
    21|
    22|## Pattern
    23|
    24|### Evaluation Triggers for Decline
    25|1. **No BI coverage**: At-fault driver is uninsured or has no bodily injury liability coverage
    26|2. **No UM/UIM coverage**: Client's own policy does not include uninsured/underinsured motorist coverage
    27|3. **PIP-only recovery**: Only available coverage is PIP (medical payments), which does not include pain and suffering
    28|4. **UM rejection on file**: Client's insurer can produce a signed UM rejection form
    29|5. **Case manager flags the issue**: "No BI or UM only PIP through KAC" — paralegal identifies the coverage gap and flags for attorney
    30|6. **Client at fault**: Police report or investigation reveals the firm's client was the at-fault party, eliminating any BI recovery path
    31|7. **No household insurance**: Attorney directs checking the client's own and household insurance; if none exists and no BI available, decline
    32|
    33|### Decline Authorization Process
    34|1. Case manager identifies the coverage gap
    35|2. Case manager requests attorney permission to decline ("@attorney permission to decline")
    36|3. Attorney reviews and authorizes
    37|4. This is a required attorney review trigger — paralegals cannot unilaterally close a case
    38|
    39|### Closing Sequence
    40|1. **Phase Change**: Status changed to "Closing"
    41|2. **Closing Letter**: Sent to the client explaining the firm is withdrawing representation
    42|3. **Carrier Notification**: Closing letters sent to BI carrier, PIP carrier, and all lien holders
    43|4. **Document Preservation**: All documents moved to permanent storage (e.g., GDrive)
    44|5. **Final Confirmation**: Attorney confirms the closing is complete
    45|
    46|### Extended File Setup Before Decline
    47|Cases that are ultimately declined may spend months in File Setup:
    48|- Client may be unreachable for weeks, preventing completion of intake tasks
    49|- Police report requests (ORR) may take months to process
    50|- Insurance investigation reveals coverage gaps only after detailed review
    51|- Medical records may still be collected during this period (providers like Air Evac)
    52|
    53|## Evidence
    54|
    55|- In an auto accident case (DOI 3/2024), the case was opened in 9/2024 and spent nearly a year in File Setup. The client was difficult to reach (voicemail full on initial contact attempts). Investigation revealed the at-fault driver had no BI or UM coverage — only PIP through KAC was available. The case manager flagged this: "update if we are declining client? No BI or UM only PIP through KAC." After requesting and receiving attorney permission to decline, the attorney sent a closing letter and instructed staff to notify all parties. Despite the case being closed, a police report ORR to the local PD was still pending and took additional months to complete.
    56|- In a case where the client was at fault (DOI mid-2025, KY), the police report confirmed liability against the firm's client. The paralegal noted "CLIENT AT FAULT" in the file and asked the attorney: "ok to decline and close? client at fault." This represents a distinct decline trigger — not coverage gaps but affirmative client fault determined after file setup.
    57|- In another case (DOI 4/2025, KY), the attorney directed the paralegal to "double check and see if [client] has any insurance or household insurance. If not set up KAC then decline and close." This shows the investigatory step before decline — exhausting all possible coverage sources (client's own, household members) before making the decline decision.
    58|
    59|## Agent Guidance
    60|
    61|1. During file setup, verify BI and UM/UIM coverage as early as possible — this is the primary gate for case viability
    62|2. If no BI and no UM/UIM is available, flag immediately for attorney review — do not continue spending resources on the case
    63|3. Request UM rejection documentation from the client's carrier to confirm
    64|4. When the attorney authorizes a decline, follow the complete closing sequence: closing letter to client, copies to all carriers and lien holders
    65|5. Move all case documents to permanent storage before closing
    66|6. Even on declined cases, complete any pending ORR/records requests — the records may be needed if the client retains another firm
    67|
    68|## Pitfalls
    69|
    70|- Spending months working a case before discovering there is no BI or UM coverage — verify coverage in the first week
    71|- Not requesting UM rejection documentation from the client's insurer
    72|- Closing a case without notifying all parties (carriers, lien holders)
    73|- Not preserving case documents before closing
    74|- Client's voicemail being full for weeks — use text and email as backup channels immediately
    75|- Allowing SOL countdown to continue without action on a case that should have been declined months earlier
    76|
    77|## Related
    78|
    79|- [[intake-and-onboarding-workflow]] — coverage verification is part of intake
    80|- [[case-phase-transitions]] — decline is a terminal phase transition
    81|- [[attorney-review-triggers]] — case decline requires attorney authorization
    82|- [[uninsured-motorist-pip-only-cases]] — the coverage gap that triggers most declines

     7|confidence: high

     9|created: 2026-04-12

    16|---
    17|
    18|# Health Insurance Lien Management
    19|
    20|> When a PI client has health insurance that pays accident-related medical bills, the health insurer asserts a subrogation lien — a right to be reimbursed from any settlement. Managing these liens involves verifying their accuracy, removing unrelated charges, and ultimately negotiating a reduction. Liens directly reduce the client's net recovery.
    21|
    22|## Pattern
    23|
    24|The health insurance lien lifecycle:
    25|
    26|1. **Identification**: The health insurer sends a "notice of subrogation claim" or "subrogation interest" letter once they learn of the accident
    27|2. **Itemization Request**: Request a detailed itemization of the lien showing each date of service and amount paid
    28|3. **Accuracy Audit**: Compare the lien itemization against the case's actual accident-related treatment
    29|4. **Dispute Unrelated Charges**: If the lien includes charges for treatment unrelated to the accident (pre-existing conditions, unrelated medical visits), request removal with supporting documentation
    30|5. **Updated Ledger**: Once disputed items are removed, obtain an updated lien ledger
    31|6. **Settlement Integration**: Factor the adjusted lien into the settlement math
    32|7. **Negotiation**: After settlement, negotiate a reduction of the lien (typically firms negotiate a percentage reduction)
    33|
    34|## Evidence
    35|
    36|- In Case C, the health insurer (Anthem) asserted a subrogation lien that included a 5/16/2024 date of service for an angiogram. The attorney identified that this procedure was scheduled prior to the accident (on 4/10/2024) for a pre-existing cardiac condition and was unrelated to the MVA. The paralegal emailed the lien handler (Carelon) requesting removal of the unrelated dates of service. An updated lien ledger with the charges removed was received the same day.
    37|- In Case D (chain-reaction MVA with 20+ providers), the case accumulated 6+ liens including health insurance (Optum at $1,707), provider liens (Cutting Edge Orthopedics at $25,763.75), and a hospital funding lien (Plano Surgical Hospital Funding LLC at $2,553.70). Multiple "Unknown" lien holders were also identified, illustrating that lien tracking becomes exponentially harder with more providers. Provider liens (as distinct from health insurance liens) may come from providers who treated on a lien basis.
    38|- In Case F (auto accident, dental injury), Anthem Blue Cross Blue Shield and Anthem Medicaid both asserted liens of $425.41 on the same case. The firm used ZipLiens to track the health insurance lien but initially sent the request to the wrong entity. Delta Dental of Kentucky also asserted a lien (amount unconfirmed). This demonstrates that dental injury cases can involve dental insurance liens in addition to health insurance liens — a distinct lien type not seen in standard MVA cases.
    39|- In Case G (auto accident, UIM litigation), Humana asserted a health insurance subrogation lien of $11,809. The case also involved pre-settlement funding ($31,200 from Legal Funding Partners), illustrating how the combination of health insurance liens plus funding can dramatically reduce client net recovery.
    40|- In Case H (auto accident, Medicaid), a Conduent lien of $20,669.08 was identified. Conduent is the third-party administrator for Kentucky Medicaid subrogation. The firm sent a first lien letter to Conduent at intake and later provided the Conduent lien information to the KAC adjuster during the claims process. Conduent called the firm to follow up on the case status, demonstrating that Medicaid subrogation administrators actively monitor cases.
    41|- In Case M (MVA, multi-plaintiff), multiple health insurance liens were identified early: Anthem BCBS, a managed care provider, and a pain management provider lien. The lien request was faxed to the health insurer on the day of file setup. Additionally, multiple "Unknown" lien holders appeared in the lien tracker, demonstrating that in cases with 6+ medical providers, identifying all subrogation interests takes extended effort. The case's policy limits (25/50 split among 4 plaintiffs) made lien resolution particularly critical since the per-plaintiff recovery was limited.
    42|- In Case N (premises liability estate, DOI 7/2020), a health insurance lien was identified from Humana at $5,887.97 (amount confirmed). Three additional "Unknown" lien holders were also listed. The Humana lien was identified early in the case via the case summary. The confirmed lien amount combined with the estate nature of the case (deceased client) means lien resolution will impact the surviving spouse's net recovery. This case further confirms the pattern that health insurance liens must be identified at intake and tracked throughout the case — even in litigation that spans 5+ years.
    43|- In a wrongful death MVA case (DOI 8/2023, KY), a union benefit trust (TeamCare) asserted a health insurance lien of $89,212.31. A second lien from a BCBS plan was also identified. The TeamCare lien required calling the trust's main line (800 number) and escalating when the member's status was flagged as "not active." The firm spoke with trust representatives multiple times before the status was being reinstated. The lien verification service Zipliens was used to confirm amounts. This case illustrates that union/multi-employer trust benefit plan liens can be among the largest health insurance liens observed, and that trust plan administrative issues (inactive member status) can create delays in lien confirmation.
    44|- In an auto accident case (DOI 1/2024, KY), multiple liens were identified including a hospital lien from a Medicaid managed care plan ($607.41 confirmed), plus two "Unknown" lien holders and a health insurance lien from a third-party administrator. The multiplicity of unknown liens confirms the pattern that in cases with Medicaid managed care, multiple subrogation entities may be involved (the MCO itself, the state Medicaid program, and third-party administrators).
    45|- In an auto accident case (DOI 9/2021, KY), a Medicaid lien of $2,452.30 was confirmed from a state Medicaid managed care plan. An additional "Unknown" health insurance lien was also present. Despite extensive treatment ($176K+ in medical bills), the Medicaid lien was relatively modest — confirming that Medicaid reimbursement rates result in smaller liens relative to total billing.
    46|
    47|## Agent Guidance
    48|
    49|1. When a subrogation notice arrives, log it immediately and request a detailed itemization
    50|2. Compare every date of service on the lien against the client's accident-related treatment dates
    51|3. Flag any dates of service that fall outside the treatment window or relate to unrelated conditions
    52|4. Draft a dispute letter to the lien handler citing the specific dates and reasons for removal
    53|5. Obtain pre-accident medical records if needed to prove a condition was pre-existing
    54|6. Keep the updated lien ledger in the case file for settlement calculations
    55|7. Alert the attorney whenever the lien amount changes materially
    56|
    57|## Pitfalls
    58|
    59|- Accepting the lien amount at face value without auditing it
    60|- Not knowing the client's pre-existing medical history (makes it impossible to identify unrelated charges)
    61|- Delaying the lien dispute — the longer you wait, the harder it is to resolve before settlement
    62|- Confusing the health insurance lien with PIP payments (they are separate obligations)
    63|- Not getting the updated lien ledger in writing after charges are removed
    64|
    65|### Government vs. Private Liens
    66|
    67|Note: Government entity liens (VA, Medicare, Medicaid) follow fundamentally different processes than private health insurance liens. See [[va-lien-management]] for the VA-specific lien process. Key differences include federal legal authority, longer timelines, and less flexibility in negotiation.
    68|
    69|## Related
    70|
    71|- [[pip-claims-management]] — PIP payments reduce what health insurance pays (and thus the lien)
    72|- [[demand-value-factors]] — the lien amount affects net recovery and settlement strategy
    73|- [[pre-existing-condition-separation]] — separating accident-related from pre-existing treatment
    74|- [[va-lien-management]] — VA liens are a distinct category requiring different handling
    75|
    76|### Batch 3 Evidence (6 additional cases)
    77|
    78|- In 5+ cases, ZipLiens was used as the standard lien verification and resolution service. ZipLiens processed Medicaid liens, Anthem/Carelon liens, and sent automated notifications when liens were processed. The firm relied on ZipLiens for lien identification, amount verification, and negotiation.
    79|- In a case (DOI 2/2023, KY), after settlement the Anthem lien payment of $4,664.54 was mailed by check from the firm. The lien was tracked by Anthem file number.
    80|- In a case (DOI 4/2023, KY), the Medicaid lien payment of $2,526.69 was mailed to Carelon (Anthem's subrogation entity). Carelon subsequently reached out for a "status update on the distribution of the settlement funds" when the payment had not been received, and the firm's ZipLiens file remained open.
    81|- In a case (DOI 5/2022, KY), Optum called asking for an update on lien payment after PIP was exhausted. The firm asked the analyst to send the updated lien with reduction — confirming that lien amounts are negotiated downward post-settlement.
    82|- In a settled case (DOI 11/2023, KY), med pay from the at-fault carrier's policy was used specifically to cover the health insurance lien: "Settled at $15,000. Need to use Med Pay from policy to cover health lien." This confirms med pay as a strategic tool for lien resolution.
    83|- In a multi-track case, the BI carrier specified "settled inclusive of all liens" vs "exclusive of PIP" — these settlement terms directly affect how liens are allocated in the disbursement.
    84|
    85|### Batch 5 Evidence (5 supplementary cases)
    86|
    87|- In a Medicaid case (DOI 6/2021, KY, minor child), Discovery Health Partners (MDwise/Medicaid managed care) asserted a lien. The firm faxed a "Final Health Lien Request" to Discovery Health Partners and followed up repeatedly over months (Nov 2022 through Nov 2022+). The Conduent claim number was tracked. The inability to obtain the final lien amount from Conduent/Medicaid over multiple months is a persistent pattern — in this case, repeated tasks were set to follow up with "Have we received the Final lien from Conduent? If not follow up." This demonstrates that Medicaid lien confirmation can stall settlement disbursement for months.
    88|- In a MVA case (DOI 2/2022, KY, litigation->settlement), Conduent (Kentucky Medicaid subrogation administrator) proactively contacted the firm multiple times requesting lien status: first via email in Aug 2025 ("We sent you a notice of our lien... kindly advise us of the status"), then by phone in Jan 2026. The lien remained unresolved 8+ months after the case moved to settlement phase. This confirms that Conduent actively monitors and pursues lien recovery on settled cases, and prolonged non-response creates compliance risk.
    89|", "total_lines": 88, "file_size": 11313, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

    16|---
    17|
    18|# Post-Settlement Provider and Lien Disputes
    19|
    20|> After settlement funds are disbursed, providers and lien holders may contact the firm months or even years later with payment disputes. These disputes include underpayment claims (interest not included), lost checks requiring reissuance, and providers threatening to pursue the client directly through the court system. This creates ongoing firm liability and client anxiety well after the case is considered closed.
    21|
    22|## Pattern
    23|
    24|1. **Settlement Completed**: Case is settled, checks mailed, client paid
    25|2. **Provider/Lien Holder Contacts Firm**: Months later, a provider or lien holder contacts the firm claiming underpayment or non-receipt
    26|3. **Escalation**: Provider leaves repeated voicemails, sends faxes, and threatens alternative collection routes (court filing against client)
    27|4. **Investigation**: Firm must investigate what was paid, what was agreed, and whether additional payment is owed
    28|5. **Resolution**: Additional check issued, original check traced, or provider redirected
    29|
    30|### Common Post-Settlement Dispute Types
    31|
    32|- **Interest not included in lien payment**: Provider claims interest was promised but not paid
    33|- **Check not received**: Conduent or other lien holder did not receive the mailed check, requiring reissuance
    34|- **Provider balance remaining**: Treatment costs exceeded PIP/lien payments plus settlement allocation
    35|- **Funeral home balance**: In wrongful death cases, funeral home providers may claim unpaid balances years after settlement
    36|
    37|## Evidence
    38|
    39|- In a KY MVA case (settled), a funeral home provider left repeated voicemails months after settlement: "I sent in some information showing where the interest was promised... the interest did not get taken care of." The provider escalated: "if there is no funds available... I need to try a different avenue" and threatened to "file a settlement with the court systems." The provider called multiple times over weeks, requesting either a check for the difference or notification that no more funds would be forthcoming.
    40|- In a KY MVA case (DOI 11/2022, KY), the Conduent Medicaid lien payment check was not received. The firm had to mail a second check: "check #7685 (#2) mailed to Conduent" — confirming that lien payment checks can be lost in transit and require re-issuance with new check numbers.
    41|- In a KY MVA case (DOI 8/2022, KY), Conduent called "looking for lien funds" months after the case settled, indicating the firm had not yet paid the Medicaid lien. The firm then mailed a check for $1,250.67 against a $12,000+ lien.
    42|- In a KY MVA case (DOI 12/2023, KY), a Medicaid subrogation analyst sent urgent repeated requests: "SECOND REQUEST - PLEASE ANSWER TODAY CASE SETTLED - KY MEDICAID LIEN HAS NOT BEEN [paid]" — demonstrating that delayed lien payments after settlement create aggressive follow-up.
    43|- Post-settlement provider complaints were observed in 5+ cases where providers or lien holders contacted the firm after settlement to request payment or dispute amounts.
    44|
    45|## Agent Guidance
    46|
    47|1. Before closing a case file, verify ALL lien payments have been sent AND received (track check clearance)
    48|2. Document any interest or balance agreements with providers in writing before settlement
    49|3. When mailing lien checks, use tracking/delivery confirmation to avoid lost-check disputes
    50|4. Set a 90-day post-settlement follow-up task to verify all checks have cleared
    51|5. When a provider contacts post-settlement, pull the disbursement sheet immediately and compare to claimed amounts
    52|6. Escalate all post-settlement disputes to the attorney for guidance before making additional payments
    53|
    54|## Pitfalls
    55|
    56|- Not tracking check clearance after mailing lien payments
    57|- Not documenting interest or balance agreements with providers before settlement
    58|- Ignoring post-settlement provider calls (they will escalate to legal action against the client)
    59|- Assuming "case closed" means no further financial obligations
    60|- Not keeping disbursement records accessible after case closure
    61|
    62|## Related
    63|
    64|- [[settlement-disbursement-workflow]] — the disbursement process that precedes these disputes
    65|- [[lien-resolution-delays-disbursement]] — how unresolved liens create the conditions for these disputes
    66|- [[medicaid-lien-management]] — Conduent/Medicaid liens are frequent sources of post-settlement disputes
    67|- [[client-frustration-escalation]] — post-settlement provider contact can reactivate client frustration
    68|", "total_lines": 67, "file_size": 4969, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

    16|---
    17|
    18|# Medicaid Lien Management (Conduent)
    19|
    20|> When a PI client is covered by Medicaid, the state asserts a subrogation lien for accident-related medical expenses paid by Medicaid. In Kentucky, Medicaid subrogation is administered by Conduent (a third-party administrator). Medicaid liens differ from private health insurance liens in that they involve government authority, can be substantial (covering hospital stays and emergency treatment), and the lien administrator actively monitors cases and follows up with law firms.
    21|
    22|## Pattern
    23|
    24|The Medicaid lien lifecycle:
    25|
    26|1. **Early Identification**: At intake, determine whether the client has Medicaid coverage. If yes, send a first lien letter to the Medicaid subrogation administrator (Conduent in Kentucky) immediately.
    27|2. **Lien Assertion**: Conduent will respond with a lien amount based on Medicaid payments made for accident-related treatment. Response times are typically faster than VA but slower than private insurers.
    28|3. **Active Monitoring**: Unlike private health insurance lien holders who are largely passive, Conduent actively monitors cases — they will call the firm to check on case status.
    29|4. **Lien Coordination**: Medicaid lien amounts must be coordinated with other payment sources (PIP, KAC, BI settlement). Bills paid by Medicaid cannot also be claimed in the demand as unpaid specials.
    30|5. **Dual Lien Possibility**: A client may have both Medicaid (Anthem Medicaid) and private health insurance (Anthem BCBS) liens on the same case — these are separate entities requiring separate tracking.
    31|
    32|### Key Differences from Private Health Insurance Liens
    33|
    34|| Aspect | Private Health Insurance | Medicaid (Conduent) |
    35||--------|------------------------|---------------------|
    36|| Legal basis | State subrogation law / ERISA | Federal/state Medicaid law |
    37|| Administrator | Insurance company's subrogation dept | Conduent (third-party administrator) |
    38|| Monitoring | Generally passive | Active — calls firm to check status |
    39|| Lien amounts | Typically moderate | Can be very large (hospital stays covered) |
    40|| Negotiation | Percentage reductions common | Government program — less flexibility |
    41|| Coordination | With PIP/settlement | With PIP, KAC, and settlement |
    42|
    43|## Evidence
    44|
    45|- In Case F (auto accident, dental injury), both Anthem Blue Cross Blue Shield and Anthem Medicaid asserted liens of $425.41 on the same case. The firm tracked these as separate liens, using ZipLiens for the health insurance lien. The dual-lien scenario required the firm to identify which entity was the correct recipient for lien correspondence — an error in routing (sending to the wrong Anthem entity) caused delays. This demonstrates that "Anthem" can represent both private insurance AND Medicaid, and the firm must distinguish between them.
    46|- In Case H (auto accident, stolen vehicle/coverage denial), Conduent asserted a Medicaid lien of $20,669.08 — the largest single lien observed in the case. The firm sent a "First Lien Letter faxed to Conduent" at file setup. Conduent later called the firm to follow up on case status (voicemail from Conduent on 2/5/2025). The firm provided the Conduent lien information and medical records/bills to the KAC adjuster during the claims process. The substantial Medicaid lien (covering a multi-day hospital stay at the University of Louisville Hospital) illustrates that Medicaid lien amounts can be proportionally larger than private insurance liens because Medicaid pays a higher share of hospital costs for low-income clients.
    47|- In an MVA case (DOI 8/2024, KY), Conduent asserted a Medicaid lien of $3,830 (amount confirmed). The client had ongoing dental treatment at a university dental school. Conduent called the firm to check on case status — the note read: "Conduent called for status of her case — case is still open — client still having dental treatment." The adjuster from the BI carrier (Progressive) also called to get treatment status information. This case confirms the active-monitoring pattern: Conduent proactively calls for case status updates, unlike private insurers who typically wait passively. The firm must be prepared to provide status updates to both the BI carrier and the Medicaid lien holder concurrently.
    48|
    49|## Agent Guidance
    50|
    51|1. At intake, ask whether the client has Medicaid coverage. If yes, flag the case as "Medicaid lien likely"
    52|2. Send a first lien letter to Conduent immediately during file setup — do not delay
    53|3. Track the Medicaid lien SEPARATELY from any private health insurance lien, even if both are through "Anthem"
    54|4. When Conduent calls to check on case status, respond promptly — they are actively monitoring
    55|5. Before preparing the demand or settlement math, obtain the current Medicaid lien amount from Conduent
    56|6. Coordinate Medicaid lien payments with PIP and KAC payments — ensure no double-billing
    57|7. When presenting settlement options to the attorney, include the Medicaid lien as a mandatory deduction
    58|8. Use ZipLiens or similar tracking tools to monitor Medicaid liens alongside health insurance liens
    59|
    60|## Pitfalls
    61|
    62|- Confusing Anthem Medicaid with Anthem BCBS (they are separate entities with different lien processes)
    63|- Not sending the first lien letter to Conduent at file setup (delays lien identification)
    64|- Not responding to Conduent's follow-up calls (they may escalate or assert incorrect lien amounts)
    65|- Not factoring the Medicaid lien into settlement math early (Medicaid liens can be the largest single deduction)
    66|- Assuming Medicaid liens can be negotiated down as easily as private health insurance liens
    67|- Not tracking Medicaid lien payments alongside KAC/PIP payments
    68|
    69|## Related
    70|
    71|- [[lien-management-health-insurance]] — contrast with private health insurance lien process
    72|- [[va-lien-management]] — another government lien with distinct handling
    73|- [[pip-claims-management]] — PIP payments may offset Medicaid lien obligations
    74|- [[kentucky-assigned-claims-process]] — KAC payments interact with Medicaid liens
    75|
    76|### Batch 4 Evidence (23 Medicaid cases)
    77|
    78|- In 23 cases from this batch, Medicaid/Conduent interactions were documented. Conduent was the most common Medicaid subrogation administrator, with cases spanning first lien letter faxing, final lien requests, and lien payment.
    79|- In a case (DOI 11/2022, KY), Conduent requested updated payment status months after settlement: "I just received an email from Conduent requesting an update on payment since it has been a few months." The firm then mailed check to Conduent. A second check had to be mailed when the first was not received — demonstrating that Conduent lien payment requires tracking and follow-up.
    80|- In a case (DOI 8/2022, KY), Conduent called "looking for lien funds" — the lien amount remained at $12,000+. The firm mailed a negotiated check for $1,250.67, confirming that Medicaid liens CAN be negotiated down significantly (from $12K+ to $1.25K, approximately 10% of the asserted amount).
    81|- In a case (DOI 7/2023, KY), the Medicaid lien was sent to PIP carrier with instructions to pay: "Send the Medicaid lien to PIP, Kentucky Farm Bureau with instructions to pay." This confirms that PIP carriers can pay Medicaid liens directly, reducing the firm's lien resolution burden.
    82|- In a case (DOI 12/2023, KY), Conduent (Medicaid subrogation analyst) sent repeated urgent requests: "SECOND REQUEST - PLEASE ANSWER TODAY CASE SETTLED - KY MEDICAID LIEN HAS NOT BEEN [paid]." The aggressive follow-up pattern from Conduent confirms they actively monitor settled cases and escalate when payment is delayed.
    83|- In a case (DOI 11/2023, KY), Conduent adjuster correspondence showed detailed email exchanges about lien amounts and payment timing, with the adjuster (Kristy Caldwell) maintaining consistent follow-up.
    84|- In 5+ cases, "First Lien Letter faxed to Conduent" was documented during file setup, confirming the standard workflow of early Medicaid lien identification.
    85|- The Medicaid lien amounts observed ranged from small ($73.48, $211.36) to substantial ($3,047.28, $12,000+), depending on the extent of Medicaid-covered treatment.

    15|---
    16|
    17|# Medicare Lien Management
    18|
    19|> Medicare conditional payment liens represent a federal government interest in recovering payments made for accident-related treatment. Unlike private health insurance subrogation, Medicare liens are governed by the Medicare Secondary Payer (MSP) Act and carry federal legal authority. The presence of a Medicare lien adds complexity to settlement calculations and lien resolution timelines.
    20|
    21|## Pattern
    22|
    23|### Medicare Lien Lifecycle
    24|1. **Identification**: Medicare is identified as a payer during records/billing review
    25|2. **Conditional Payment Notice**: Medicare/CMS issues a conditional payment letter quantifying amounts paid
    26|3. **Amount Confirmation**: Firm verifies the conditional payment amount against treatment records
    27|4. **Demand Impact**: Medicare lien amount must be factored into demand calculations
    28|5. **Resolution**: After settlement, firm must satisfy Medicare's conditional payment claim before disbursing to client
    29|6. **Potential Negotiation**: Unlike VA liens, Medicare conditional payments may be subject to reduction through the BCRC (Benefits Coordination & Recovery Center) process
    30|
    31|### Key Characteristics
    32|- Federal legal authority (MSP Act) — cannot be ignored
    33|- Conditional payment amounts may include treatment unrelated to the accident — requires audit
    34|- CMS/BCRC is the counterparty, not a local office
    35|- Timeline for final demand letter from CMS can extend months post-settlement
    36|- Overlaps with other liens (Medicaid/Wellcare) creating complex multi-lien scenarios
    37|
    38|## Evidence
    39|
    40|- In Case H (MVA, air evac), a Medicare conditional payment lien was confirmed at $10,157.36 (notice received 5/17/2024). The case also had a Wellcare of Kentucky (Medicaid managed care) lien at $2,315.49 and an unidentified health insurance lien. The multi-lien landscape (Medicare + Medicaid managed care + unknown) created significant complexity for settlement math. The air evacuation bill alone was $72K+, meaning Medicare's conditional payment was a fraction of total treatment costs but still substantial enough to impact net recovery.
    41|- In Case S (auto accident, government transit defendant, DOI 9/2021), Medicare was identified as a lien holder alongside a Humana health insurance lien ($1,513.45). The client was a Medicare beneficiary with treatment at multiple Louisville-area hospitals and EMS providers. The case involved a government entity defendant (transit risk management), creating a multi-layer complexity: federal Medicare lien + private health insurance lien + government tort claim. The Medicare lien remained in "identified" status throughout the observed activity, confirming the pattern that Medicare liens are often identified early but final amounts take months to confirm. The coexistence of Medicare and Humana liens on the same case confirms that Medicare beneficiaries may have supplemental private insurance, each asserting separate subrogation interests.
    42|- In Case U (auto accident, employer liability/taxi company, DOI 9/2024), Medicare liens from multiple entities were identified: Aetna Medicare and Medicare BCRC (Benefits Coordination & Recovery Center), plus additional "Unknown" Medicare liens. The client was treated at a regional medical center with $31K+ in billings. The presence of both Aetna Medicare (a Medicare Advantage plan) and traditional Medicare BCRC on the same case demonstrates that Medicare Advantage plans assert liens separately from CMS. This creates a dual-Medicare-lien scenario requiring the firm to track and resolve both independently. The case also involved bifurcated litigation issues, adding procedural complexity on top of the lien resolution challenge.
    43|
    44|## Agent Guidance
    45|
    46|1. During intake and records review, determine whether the client has Medicare coverage
    47|2. If Medicare paid for any accident-related treatment, initiate a conditional payment query with BCRC early
    48|3. Track the conditional payment amount as a confirmed lien in the case file
    49|4. Audit the conditional payment letter against treatment records — challenge any unrelated charges
    50|5. Factor Medicare lien into demand calculations and settlement evaluation
    51|6. After settlement, submit the final demand to BCRC and negotiate reduction if applicable
    52|7. Do NOT disburse settlement funds until Medicare's final demand is resolved
    53|8. When Medicare coexists with other liens (Medicaid, private), calculate total lien exposure before evaluating any offer
    54|
    55|## Pitfalls
    56|
    57|- Not identifying Medicare as a payer early in the case
    58|- Assuming Medicare liens can be ignored or informally resolved
    59|- Not auditing conditional payment amounts for unrelated treatment charges
    60|- Disbursing settlement funds before Medicare's final demand is issued
    61|- Confusing Medicare conditional payments with Medicaid/managed care liens (different processes)
    62|- Not factoring total lien exposure (Medicare + Medicaid + private) into settlement evaluation
    63|
    64|## Related
    65|
    66|- [[lien-management-health-insurance]] — private health insurance subrogation follows different rules
    67|- [[va-lien-management]] — VA liens share the government lien characteristic but use OGC process
    68|- [[demand-preparation-workflow]] — Medicare lien must be identified before demand is sent
    69|", "total_lines": 68, "file_size": 5603, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

    15|---
    16|
    17|# Pre-Settlement Funding Obligations
    18|
    19|> Some PI clients obtain "pre-settlement funding" (lawsuit loans) from third-party lenders while their case is pending. These loans create obligations that must be satisfied from the settlement proceeds, reducing the client's net recovery. The firm must understand its obligation to the funder and factor the loan into settlement math.
    20|
    21|## Pattern
    22|
    23|Pre-settlement funding creates a complex financial picture at settlement:
    24|
    25|1. Client takes a loan from a funding company (e.g., "Claims Angel") during the pendency of the case
    26|2. The loan accrues interest and fees over time
    27|3. At settlement, the funding must be repaid from the client's share of proceeds
    28|4. The attorney must factor the loan payoff into the disbursement sheet
    29|
    30|Settlement math with pre-settlement funding:
    31|- Gross settlement
    32|- Less: Attorney fees
    33|- Less: Case costs
    34|- Less: Medical provider payments/liens
    35|- Less: Pre-settlement funding payoff
    36|- Equals: Client's net recovery
    37|
    38|When the settlement offer is modest, pre-settlement funding can consume a large portion of what's left, leaving the client with very little.
    39|
    40|## Evidence
    41|
    42|- In Case A, the client had "loans" from a company named "Claims Angel" totaling $6,537.05. With an $18K offer, after paying the loan ($6,537.05) and 50% of the medical provider bill ($5,397.50), and attorney fees, the client would receive less than $3K. The paralegal noted: "I'm not sure what obligation we have to satisfy the presettlement funding."
    43|- In Case D (long-running litigation/negotiation), a loan contract was sent to the client in May 2025 — approximately 2.5 years after the accident. The funding occurred during active litigation while awaiting IMEs and depositions, illustrating that funding pressure increases the longer a case takes to resolve.
    44|- In Case G (auto accident, UIM litigation), a $31,200 lien from Legal Funding Partners was identified. The case involved extensive treatment ($45K+ hospital bills alone) and a UIM claim, suggesting the client sought funding during the prolonged treatment/litigation period. The high funding amount relative to case value demonstrates the risk of pre-settlement funding in complex cases — it can consume a large portion of the recovery.
    45|- In Case G, the client's expenses included $5,000 listed as a payment to the client, plus $500 for EvenUP and $495 for Digital Owl (case evaluation tools), illustrating the range of costs that accumulate during complex litigation beyond just the funding itself.
    46|
    47|## Agent Guidance
    48|
    49|1. During intake, ask if the client has obtained any pre-settlement funding or lawsuit loans
    50|2. If yes, request the funding agreement and current payoff amount
    51|3. Log the funder name, original loan amount, and current payoff in the case financial records
    52|4. Factor the funding payoff into all settlement evaluations
    53|5. When presenting settlement options to the client, show the complete disbursement breakdown including the funding payoff
    54|6. Ask the attorney about the firm's legal obligation to satisfy the funding (varies by jurisdiction and agreement)
    55|7. If the settlement barely covers obligations, flag this to the attorney early
    56|
    57|## Pitfalls
    58|
    59|- Not discovering the pre-settlement funding until settlement disbursement
    60|- Not understanding the firm's legal obligation to the funder
    61|- Not factoring funding into the settlement evaluation, leading to unrealistic client expectations
    62|- Assuming the payoff amount is the same as the original loan (interest and fees accrue)
    63|
    64|## Related
    65|
    66|- [[settlement-offer-evaluation]] — funding obligations affect whether an offer is adequate
    67|- [[demand-value-factors]] — knowing the funding obligation influences demand strategy
    68|- [[lien-management-health-insurance]] — liens and funding together determine net recovery
    69|", "total_lines": 68, "file_size": 4207, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}
