    16|  - multi-carrier-negotiation
    17|---
    18|
    19|# PIP Claims Management
    20|
    21|> Personal Injury Protection (PIP) is a first-party insurance benefit (from the client's own policy or the at-fault party's policy in KY) that covers medical bills regardless of fault. Managing PIP claims is a critical parallel workflow that runs alongside the BI claim. PIP must be actively directed to pay bills, and its ledger must be tracked for demand preparation.
    22|
    23|## Pattern
    24|
    25|PIP management follows this lifecycle:
    26|
    27|1. **Setup**: Send Letter of Representation to PIP carrier with claim number during file setup
    28|2. **Bill Routing**: As medical bills come in, forward them to PIP with a "direction to pay" (explicit instruction to process)
    29|3. **Ledger Tracking**: Periodically request PIP ledger updates to track what has been paid
    30|4. **Demand Integration**: The PIP ledger is needed before sending the BI demand to understand total medical specials and what remains unpaid
    31|5. **Exhaust Monitoring**: In some cases, PIP benefits may exhaust before all bills are paid
    32|
    33|Key observations from case data:
    34|- PIP carriers may sit on submitted bills for extended periods (30+ days)
    35|- Bills sometimes need to be re-sent when PIP does not process them
    36|- PIP adjusters may respond that they "still have benefits reserved" but have only one bill on file
    37|- Health insurance deductibles interact with PIP: bills may first go through health insurance, with the remainder then submitted to PIP
    38|- Indiana MVAs do NOT have PIP coverage (jurisdiction matters)
    39|
    40|## Evidence
    41|
    42|- In Case C, bills were sent to State Farm PIP on 11/1/2024, but as of 12/31/2024 (60 days later), NO PAYMENTS had been made by PIP. The attorney directed staff to resend and call PIP to identify the issue.
    43|- In Case B, the PIP adjuster from Erie Insurance responded to a ledger request noting "you still have benefits reserved" and only had one bill on file, despite multiple provider bills existing. Additional bills needed to be forwarded with direction to process.
    44|- In Case B, the PIP adjuster explained that one hospital bill was "applied to deductible" by health insurance, which is why PIP issued payment only to the radiology provider.
    45|- In Case A, the case was an Indiana MVA, meaning no PIP was available — this is a jurisdiction-specific distinction.
    46|- In Case D, PIP was used to pay a VA lien directly. The firm sent a "direction to pay" the VA lien ($4,437.13) to the PIP carrier along with the VA billing statement. The PIP adjuster issued payment directly to the Department of Veterans Affairs. This demonstrates PIP's utility beyond paying medical providers — it can satisfy government liens.
    47|- In the same case, the PIP adjuster disputed the relatedness of chiropractic treatment, noting records indicated the treatment was "related to a condition she experienced years prior" and requesting "additional documentation indicating relatedness." PIP adjusters perform their own relatedness analysis and will deny bills they consider unrelated to the accident.
    48|- In Case D (chain-reaction MVA), two separate PIP claims were set up: one with Farmers Insurance (client's own carrier) and one with American Transit Insurance (NYC cab company involved in a separate incident). The PIP application for Farmers was received and returned in early Dec 2022. The case illustrates managing PIP claims across multiple carriers when there are concurrent accidents, requiring careful tracking of which bills are attributable to which accident for PIP purposes.
    49|- In Case G (auto accident, UIM), PIP was managed through Allstate Insurance, the client's own carrier. The PIP adjuster was contacted separately from the UIM adjuster at the same carrier (different adjuster and claim number). The firm requested the ER bill copy from PIP and saved acknowledgment with the PIP log. This demonstrates the pattern where PIP and UIM are both through the same carrier but managed on separate tracks.
    50|- In Case H (auto accident, stolen vehicle), a Kentucky Assigned Claims (KAC) claim was set up through Nationwide PIP. The KAC process applies when the at-fault driver has no insurance (because coverage was denied due to the stolen vehicle). The PIP adjuster from Nationwide (Beth Freeman) managed the KAC claim, which followed a different process than standard PIP — requiring the firm to submit specific documentation and the adjuster to coordinate with other parties regarding billing disputes. The attorney agreed during an EUO that certain bills would not be submitted to KAC for payment, showing that the KAC process involves negotiation about which bills are covered.
    51|- In 13 cases from this batch (all KY MVAs, DOIs 4/2025 to 12/2025), PIP claims were set up with major carriers including State Farm, Progressive, Root Auto, Kentucky Farm Bureau, National General, Elco Insurance, Direct Auto Insurance, and SafeCo. Key patterns confirmed: (1) PIP LOR is sent on day one via fax/email to the PIP carrier; (2) PIP bills are submitted as they are received from providers with the standard request "please process the attached bill"; (3) PIP ledger requests are a recurring task — "please forward an updated PIP ledger" or "please forward a copy of the most recent PIP ledger" was observed in 8+ cases; (4) Same-carrier PIP and BI (e.g., Progressive handling both PIP and BI on separate claim numbers) requires careful claim number tracking; (5) In one case, the paralegal asked the PIP carrier to "process any remaining bills from [provider]" indicating batch bill submission at treatment completion.
    52|- PIP exhaustion was flagged as a concern in one case: "PIP may be exhausted. Sent request for ledger" — confirming that PIP ledger monitoring is critical to understand remaining benefits before demand preparation.
    53|- In a companion case involving 4 family members (2 adults, 2 minor children), the PIP adjuster completed the coverage investigation and communicated detailed payment decisions in a single email covering all four claimants: $4,000 reserved per person, specific billing codes denied per claimant (e.g., 97010 Hot/Cold Packs, 97014 E-stim, 97110 Therapeutic Exercise for some; 97140 Manual Therapy and 97110 for others), and ER bills excluded "per your direction." This is the most detailed PIP adjuster communication observed — showing that PIP adjusters perform individual line-item billing analysis and that the firm can direct PIP to exclude certain bill categories (such as ER bills).
    54|- In the same companion case, the PIP adjuster issued Reservation of Rights letters for the minor children because "proof of loss" could not be confirmed through the police report and claimant statement alone. Recorded interviews with the adult passengers were required as alternate proof of loss. This demonstrates that PIP coverage for passengers (especially minors) may require additional verification beyond the police report.
    55|- In a case with no PIP coverage available ("NO TREATMENT TO DATE AND NO PIP"), the BI claim proceeded independently without any PIP track. This confirms the jurisdiction-conditional nature of PIP: even in Kentucky, individual cases may lack PIP coverage if the client's own policy does not include it.
    56|- In a file-setup case, the firm faxed the PIP claim number to the carrier requesting acknowledgment within the first day of file setup, consistent with the standard LOR workflow. The carrier (Nationwide) was contacted via email to "forward the claim information or a copy of the denial of coverage" — confirming that claim status verification is done early.
    57|- In a case with an uninsured at-fault driver and no BI available, the attorney directed staff to check for household insurance before setting up KAC PIP — confirming the coverage investigation hierarchy: BI first, then UM/UIM, then household insurance, then KAC as last resort.
    58|- In 8 additional small MVA cases (KY, DOIs 6/2025-2/2026), PIP management was observed across State Farm (4 cases), Progressive (1), Liberty Mutual (1), National General (1), and Root Auto Insurance (1). Key observations: (1) PIP exhaustion triggers provider billing inquiries — in one case, a chiropractic/urgent care provider emailed requesting a PIP log and asking "please advise if more PIP is available or no funds till settlement"; the firm responded with a PIP exhaust letter; (2) In one case, PIP was confirmed as "plenty of PIP remaining" and the firm instructed the provider to resubmit billing directly to PIP; (3) State Farm PIP was managed through a centralized PIP team (separate from the BI adjuster) accessible via a dedicated phone extension; (4) One case with Root Auto Insurance as PIP carrier and a different carrier for BI confirmed the pattern of split-carrier PIP/BI management; (5) In one case, the PIP carrier (Progressive) requested recorded statements as part of a "proof of loss" investigation for PIP claims, scheduling it through the firm — this is distinct from the BI recorded statement and was handled as a separate event.
    59|- In one case (DOI 2/2026), the firm noted "NO PIP COVERAGE" at intake on the same day. The case proceeded with BI only (Travelers Insurance). This confirms that not all KY auto accident cases have PIP, and the coverage determination must be made immediately at intake. This is the second observed no-PIP case in the wiki.
    60|- Across 17 additional small cases (all KY, DOIs 5/2025-2/2026), PIP management patterns confirmed across 11 carriers: State Farm (4 cases), Kentucky Farm Bureau (3 cases), Progressive (2 cases), National General (1), Erie Insurance (1), Travelers (1), Shelter Insurance (1), Elco Insurance (1). Key confirmations: (1) PIP LOR and ledger requests sent on day one of file setup; (2) standard request language: "please forward an updated PIP ledger along with copies of any ER related records/bills received to date"; (3) PIP ledger requests sent to carrier email/fax within the first 2 weeks; (4) in one case, the paralegal requested PIP carrier "remove any reservation for our client and process any remaining billing that is NOT ER related treatment" — confirming that PIP carriers hold reservations on benefits and the firm must direct which bills to process; (5) in a companion case pair, both companions shared the same PIP carrier and claim number (Kentucky Farm Bureau #8069631) with the same adjuster handling both; (6) Erie Insurance PIP was set up for a case with UM through Esurance — confirming that PIP and UM can be through related but distinct carrier entities; (7) one case with PIP-only (no BI identified) confirmed the coverage investigation pattern where PIP is the only available benefit.
    61|
    62|## Agent Guidance
    63|
    64|1. Check jurisdiction first — Indiana MVAs do not have PIP; Kentucky MVAs do
    65|2. During file setup, send LOR to PIP carrier and request acknowledgment
    66|3. As each medical bill is received, forward to PIP with explicit "please process the attached and forward an updated PIP ledger upon completion"
    67|4. Set a 30-day follow-up task for each bill submitted to PIP
    68|5. If no PIP payment is confirmed within 30 days, resend the bill AND call the PIP adjuster
    69|6. Request PIP ledger updates before demand preparation
    70|7. Track which bills have been paid by PIP vs. health insurance vs. unpaid
    71|8. When health insurance pays part of a bill, understand the deductible interaction before submitting to PIP
    72|
    73|## Pitfalls
    74|
    75|- Assuming PIP will automatically process bills — they require explicit direction to pay
    76|- Sending bills once and not following up for months
    77|- Not tracking which bills PIP has vs. doesn't have on file
    78|- Forgetting that out-of-state accidents may not have PIP coverage
    79|- Not understanding the health insurance deductible / PIP interaction
    80|
    81|## Related
    82|
    83|- [[medical-bill-routing-workflow]] — the overall process for routing medical bills
    84|- [[pip-ledger-requests]] — specifically requesting and tracking PIP ledgers
    85|- [[treatment-gap-defense]] — unpaid bills and PIP delays can affect case timeline
    86|
    87|### Batch 5 Evidence (5 supplementary cases)
    88|
    89|- In a passenger MVA case (DOI 1/2022, KY), PIP was through the vehicle owner's policy (Shelter Insurance), not the client's own policy. The firm sent PIP LOR on day 2 of intake and simultaneously requested a complete PIP file copy including "copies of all records and bills received to date and an updated payment ledger." A UIM claim was also opened with the client's own carrier (Nationwide) — confirming that when the client is a passenger, PIP comes from the vehicle owner's policy while UIM comes from the client's own policy.
    90|- In a self-employed client's MVA case (DOI 2/2021, KY), the PIP application was complicated: the unsigned PIP app had to be resent, and the PIP carrier (Kentucky Farm Bureau) asked whether the client was "in the process of employment when MVA happened" because WC would be primary if so. The paralegal clarified the client was self-employed (doing eyelashes). The PIP carrier also delayed lost wage reimbursement, with the client calling repeatedly about not receiving lost wages. This confirms that self-employment status creates PIP processing complications for both medical bill payment and lost wages.
    91|- In a multi-party MVA case (DOI 7/2021, KY), the PIP was through Auto Owners Insurance with a dedicated adjuster. The firm requested PIP log repeatedly ("Have we received PIP log from Auto Owners Insurance?"). The PIP track ran in parallel with a $50K Progressive BI policy tender and a Workers' Compensation claim through Sedgwick — confirming three-track parallel management (PIP + BI + WC) on a single case.
    92|", "total_lines": 91, "file_size": 13947, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

    15|---
    16|
    17|# Client Expectations Management
    18|
    19|> PI clients (and their family members) frequently become anxious about case timelines, especially when medical bills go to collections. Proactively managing expectations about the process, timeline, and their role prevents client dissatisfaction and premature settlement pressure.
    20|
    21|## Pattern
    22|
    23|Client/family anxiety follows a predictable escalation:
    24|
    25|1. **Early Phase (0-3 months)**: Client is patient, providing information as requested
    26|2. **Mid Phase (3-6 months)**: Client begins asking "Is there anything we need to be doing?" and "Are we close to resolving this?"
    27|3. **Late Phase (6+ months)**: Client receives collection notices and expresses urgency — "I don't want this to hurt our credit" and "We want this to go away"
    28|4. **Critical Point**: Client may pressure for quick settlement at any amount to end the process
    29|
    30|The firm's response pattern is typically reactive: responding to inquiries with brief status updates. Proactive communication is rare in the observed data.
    31|
    32|## Evidence
    33|
    34|- In Case B, the client's husband followed up approximately monthly with increasing urgency. His communications escalated from "Just checking in" (August) to "Do you feel like we are close?" (November) to "We received a letter from a debt collection agency... We want this to go away" (November) to "When can we reach a resolution? We really want to put this to bed" (December).
    35|- In Case A, the client's mother followed up asking "he was asking if I have heard anything about his case" after months of apparent inactivity.
    36|- In Case D, the client left repeated voicemails over a 2-month period (Nov-Dec 2025) that went unanswered, escalating from "trying to find out if there has been an update" to "I have been calling every single day and still have not had a telephone call back." The client explicitly stated "it has been over 3 years" and "I just want everything settled... with whatever" — indicating willingness to accept any amount to end the process. This is the most extreme observed case of client frustration from communication failure.
    37|- In Case D (auto accident, negotiation), the client was told the case was "on settlement phase" and that they "can't disclose the amount yet because it might still change." The client then refused the settlement, creating a confusing situation where staff noted "appears was settled but client didn't accept so maybe ongoing negotiations." This demonstrates the danger of using vague settlement language without specifics.
    38|- In Case E (premises liability, litigation spanning 3+ years), the client texted the attorney directly: "Haven't heard anything from you or an update since court on Monday, and just wanted to reach out." The attorney responded with a substantive update about next steps (records production deadline). This case shows that even in litigation, clients need regular updates after court events.
    39|
    40|## Agent Guidance
    41|
    42|1. Set expectations at intake: explain the typical case timeline (6-18 months for most PI cases)
    43|2. Send proactive status updates at least monthly, even if the update is "waiting for records"
    44|3. When bills go to collections, explain immediately: "We have submitted this to PIP for processing. It may take up to 30 days. The collection activity will be resolved through the settlement."
    45|4. When a client asks "are we close?", give a specific answer: "We need X, Y, and Z before we can send the demand. I expect that to take approximately N weeks."
    46|5. Never leave a client inquiry unanswered for more than 24 hours
    47|6. Document client expectations and concerns in the activity log
    48|
    49|## Pitfalls
    50|
    51|- Being reactive instead of proactive with client communication
    52|- Giving vague responses like "Not at this time" without explaining what the timeline looks like
    53|- Ignoring the emotional impact of collection notices on clients
    54|- Allowing months to pass without any client contact
    55|- Not explaining the process steps that remain before resolution
    56|
    57|## Related
    58|
    59|- [[client-communication-through-family]] — many of these interactions happen through family members
    60|- [[medical-bill-collections-anxiety]] — collections are the primary driver of client urgency
    61|- [[case-phase-transitions]] — understanding the phase helps set realistic expectations
    62|- [[client-frustration-escalation]] — the pattern that emerges when expectations are not managed
    63|- [[negotiation-after-demand]] — negotiation phase requires especially careful expectation management
    64|", "total_lines": 63, "file_size": 4898, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

     9|created: 2026-04-12

    16|---
    17|
    18|# Lien Resolution Delays Settlement Disbursement
    19|
    20|> Even after a settlement is reached and accepted, disbursement to the client can be delayed by weeks or months due to unresolved liens. Medicaid liens are particularly problematic because the state actively monitors and may increase lien amounts during negotiations. Third-party lien resolution services (ZipLiens) are used to manage complex lien negotiations but cannot guarantee timely resolution.
    21|
    22|## Pattern
    23|
    24|The connection between lien resolution and disbursement delay operates as follows:
    25|
    26|1. **Settlement Accepted**: Client agrees to the settlement amount
    27|2. **Lien Resolution Begins**: Firm must obtain final lien amounts from all lien holders before calculating net to client
    28|3. **Delay Factors**:
    29|   - Medicaid lien holders may increase their claimed amounts during reduction negotiations
    30|   - Health insurance lien holders may request copies of the settlement release before completing negotiations
    31|   - Out-of-state Medicaid (e.g., Ohio Medicaid) may require portal-based submissions instead of email
    32|   - Multiple liens must all be resolved before disbursement
    33|4. **Client Frustration**: Client has been told the case is settled but cannot receive their check
    34|
    35|## Evidence
    36|
    37|- In one case, Ohio Medicaid lien negotiations were managed through ZipLiens. The Medicaid program "shifted gears" and required everything previously communicated by email to be resubmitted through their portal. During reduction negotiations, Medicaid responded with a "drastically increased" lien amount rather than the expected reduction, requiring the firm to go back and verify the itemization before continuing negotiations.
    38|- In the same case, Medicaid requested a copy of the settlement release from the liability carrier before continuing lien negotiations, adding another dependency. The firm was told "it's unlikely they'll continue the negotiations until they receive it."
    39|- In an MVA case with Conduent (KY Medicaid), the lien was coordinated with PIP payments and KAC claims simultaneously, requiring the firm to track three separate payment sources against one lien.
    40|- In a case with both BI settlement and Medicaid lien, the carrier required "direct payment of any medicare/Medicaid liens and then a separate check being made to your office for the remainder" — splitting the settlement payment into multiple checks.
    41|- In 5+ cases, clients called the firm asking when they could pick up their settlement check, only to be told the check had not yet been received from the carrier or that lien resolution was still pending.
    42|- In one case, a client called months after receiving the settlement check complaining about bills still going to collections, indicating that lien resolution had either not covered all bills or had not been communicated to the client.
    43|
    44|## Agent Guidance
    45|
    46|1. Begin lien resolution immediately upon settlement acceptance — do not wait for the carrier check
    47|2. Use ZipLiens or similar services for complex lien negotiations, especially Medicaid
    48|3. Set client expectations that disbursement may take 2-6 weeks after settlement due to lien resolution
    49|4. Track all lien holders and their response status in a single dashboard
    50|5. When Medicaid increases lien amounts during negotiations, verify the itemization with the attorney before proceeding
    51|
    52|## Related
    53|
    54|- [[settlement-disbursement-workflow]] — the disbursement that lien resolution delays
    55|- [[lien-management-health-insurance]] — private health insurance lien process
    56|- [[medicaid-lien-management]] — Medicaid liens are the most common delay cause
    57|- [[pre-settlement-funding-obligations]] — funding payoffs are an additional disbursement dependency
    58|", "total_lines": 57, "file_size": 4161, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

{"content": "     1|---
     2|title: Client Frustration Drives Premature Settlement or Attorney Attrition
     3|slug: client-frustration-drives-premature-settlement
     4|phase: [phase_4_negotiation, phase_5_settlement]
     5|category: connection
     6|tags: [client-communication, frustration, settlement, attrition, negotiation]
     7|confidence: high
     8|evidence_count: 55
     9|created: 2026-04-12
    10|updated: 2026-04-12
    11|related:
    12|  - client-frustration-escalation
    13|  - client-expectations-management
    14|  - negotiation-after-demand
    15|---
    16|
    17|# Client Frustration Drives Premature Settlement or Attorney Attrition
    18|
    19|> When clients become frustrated with the pace and communication during the negotiation phase, two negative outcomes are likely: (1) the client pressures for a premature settlement at a lower value than the case warrants, or (2) the client threatens to or actually changes attorneys. Both outcomes are preventable through proactive communication and clear expectation-setting.
    20|
    21|## Connection
    22|
    23|**[[client-frustration-escalation]]** documents the 4-stage escalation pattern from polite inquiry to threats and distrust.
    24|
    25|**[[client-expectations-management]]** provides the proactive communication strategies that prevent escalation.
    26|
    27|**[[negotiation-after-demand]]** shows how premature "settlement" labeling and poor offer communication create client confusion and frustration.
    28|
    29|## Insight
    30|
    31|In the observed auto accident case, the client was told the case was "on settlement phase" but then refused the offer. Over the following 12+ months, the client made dozens of calls (many missed), sent frustrated text messages, and explicitly threatened to "take another route." Staff members were unsure of the case status ("appears was settled but client didn't accept"). The combination of poor communication, premature settlement labeling, and missed calls created a situation where the client-firm relationship was severely strained.
    32|
    33|In the premises liability case (6+ years in litigation), the client texted the attorney directly after a court appearance with no follow-up. The attorney's prompt, substantive response prevented escalation — demonstrating that responsive communication, even when there's limited news, maintains the relationship.
    34|
    35|In Case M (MVA, multi-plaintiff), the client's frustration was driven by a factor outside the firm's control: another plaintiff's counsel failing to provide medical bills needed for policy limits distribution. The client called repeatedly and was told "we are waiting on the other firm." The frustration escalated to an in-person office visit. This variant shows that inter-counsel coordination delays create the same frustration dynamic as internal delays — and the client cannot distinguish between the two.
    36|
    37|In Case N (premises liability estate, 5+ year litigation), the surviving spouse called repeatedly over 18+ months (Jul 2025-Mar 2026) with missed calls and unreturned messages. The emotional stakes are higher in estate cases where the contact is grieving. The long litigation timeline without visible progress compounds the frustration. Despite the firm actively conducting litigation (depositions, discovery), the spouse perceived inaction because he was not being proactively updated.
    38|
    39|## Agent Guidance
    40|
    41|1. Never label a case as "Settlement" until the client has agreed to accept the offer
    42|2. When an offer is received, schedule a call with the client within 48 hours to discuss
    43|3. Maintain 2-week check-in schedule during active negotiation
    44|4. When a client shows Stage 2 frustration signs, escalate to the attorney for a personal call within 24 hours
    45|5. Ensure all staff members know the current case status so they can answer client questions coherently
    46|", "total_lines": 45, "file_size": 3659, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

{"content": "     1|---
     2|title: Release Signing Delays Block PIP Carrier Reimbursement
     3|slug: release-delays-block-pip-reimbursement
     4|phase: [phase_5_settlement, phase_6_lien_resolution]
     5|category: connection
     6|tags: [release, pip, reimbursement, settlement, delay, carrier, subrogation]
     7|confidence: high
     8|evidence_count: 41
     9|created: 2026-04-12
    10|updated: 2026-04-12
    11|related:
    12|  - settlement-disbursement-workflow
    13|  - pip-claims-management
    14|  - adjuster-unreturned-calls-pattern
    15|  - adjuster-file-closure-threats
    16|---
    17|
    18|# Release Signing Delays Block PIP Carrier Reimbursement
    19|
    20|> After a BI claim settles, the at-fault carrier often owes the client's PIP carrier a reimbursement (PIP subrogation). However, the BI carrier cannot process this reimbursement until the signed release is returned. When the firm delays returning the signed release — whether due to client non-response, internal processing delays, or release term disputes — the PIP carrier is blocked from receiving its subrogation payment. This creates a cascade: the BI adjuster calls repeatedly requesting the release, the PIP adjuster may escalate, and the client may face continued billing pressure from providers who expected PIP to have been reimbursed.
    21|
    22|## How the Concepts Interact
    23|
    24|### The Causal Chain
    25|1. **[[settlement-disbursement-workflow]]**: BI settlement is agreed upon; carrier sends release
    26|2. **Release Delay**: Client doesn't sign promptly, or firm doesn't return release promptly
    27|3. **[[pip-claims-management]]**: PIP carrier is owed subrogation from BI recovery; waits for release
    28|4. **[[adjuster-unreturned-calls-pattern]]**: BI adjuster calls firm repeatedly requesting signed release
    29|5. **[[adjuster-file-closure-threats]]**: If delay persists, adjuster may escalate or close file
    30|
    31|### Why This Matters
    32|- BI carriers track release return timelines and will escalate
    33|- PIP carriers cannot be reimbursed until BI release is signed — creating inter-carrier pressure
    34|- Providers may continue billing the client or sending to collections during the delay
    35|- The firm's relationship with the carrier is damaged by non-responsiveness
    36|
    37|## Evidence
    38|
    39|- In an MVA case (DOI 7/2024, KY), the BI carrier (Travelers) settled but the firm did not return the signed release for months. The adjuster left 5+ voicemails over 2 months: "We had settled a claim... I have not been able to get a chance for [the firm] to return my call... I am trying to get a status of the signed release... I am just getting the signed release so I can pay back your client's PIP carrier." The PIP reimbursement was explicitly blocked by the missing release.
    40|- In a second case, the client was sent the release via VineSign but did not sign it. The firm resent the release multiple times. The carrier follow-up escalated from email to phone calls. The delay added weeks to the disbursement timeline.
    41|- In 3 additional cases, BI adjusters referenced PIP subrogation obligations when requesting signed releases, confirming this is a standard post-settlement dependency.
    42|
    43|## Agent Guidance
    44|
    45|1. Send releases to clients via VineSign within 24 hours of receipt from the carrier
    46|2. If the client has not signed within 48 hours, CALL the client (do not rely on email alone)
    47|3. Return signed releases to the carrier the same day they are signed
    48|4. When a BI adjuster calls about the release, provide a specific date by which it will be returned
    49|5. Track release status as a separate task — do not let it merge into general "settlement" status
    50|
    51|## Related
    52|
    53|- [[settlement-disbursement-workflow]] — the disbursement process that release delays block
    54|- [[pip-claims-management]] — PIP subrogation that depends on the release
    55|- [[adjuster-unreturned-calls-pattern]] — the adjuster behavior triggered by release delays
    56|- [[adjuster-file-closure-threats]] — escalation risk from prolonged non-response
    57|", "total_lines": 56, "file_size": 3822, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

---------- Forwarded message ---------
From: Kentucky State Police Public Records 
Date: Fri, Apr 19, 2024 at 9:11?AM
Subject: [Document Released to Requester] Kentucky State Police public
records request #24-2151
To: 


-- Attach a non-image file and/or reply ABOVE THIS LINE with a message, and
it will be sent to staff on this request. --
Kentucky State Police Public Records
A document has been released for record request #24-2151 along with the
following message:

Following a diligent search, the Kentucky State Police ("KSP") has
determined you are seeking records pertaining to Case No.16-23-0480.
Because KSP is actively investigating this incident, and the Commonwealth’s
Attorney has not declined to prosecute nor concluded prosecution regarding
it, KSP is partially denying your, request pursuant to KRS 17.150(2),
incorporated into the Open Records Act by KRS 61.878(1)(l), and
61.878(1)(h).

Pursuant to KRS 17.150(2), “[i]ntelligence and investigative reports
maintained by criminal justice agencies are subject to public inspection if
prosecution is complete or a determination not to prosecute has been made.”
KRS 61.878(1)(l) authorizes public agencies to withhold “public records or
information the disclosure of which is prohibited or restricted or
otherwise made confidential by enactment of the General Assembly.” Thus,
KRS 17.150 is incorporated into the Open Records Act by KRS 61.878(1)(l).
In 20-ORD-090, the Attorney General’s Office ruled that “the completion of
a prosecution or a decision not to prosecute is a condition precedent to
public inspection” of records within the scope of KRS 17.150(2). Further,
the Attorney General’s Office has analyzed the underlying purpose of KRS
17.150(2) and its “companion statute,” KRS 61.878(1)(h), observing that
“[i]nvestigative reports are nearly always withheld from public inspection
to protect sources of information and techniques of investigations and also
to prevent premature disclosure of the contents to the targets of
investigations, which could thwart law enforcement efforts.” 19-ORD-166
(citing OAG 83-123). Finally, the term “investigative report” is “broad
enough to extend to laboratory, forensic, and other reports generated in
the course of an investigation.” *Id*. (citing 05-ORD-246). For example,
audio and video recordings, including “dash-cam video recordings,” fall
within the parameters of KRS 17.150(2). *See *07-ORD-095 (quoting
04-ORD-234); 20-ORD-104.



The Attorney General has also evaluated KRS 17.150 in relation to
investigative materials associated with an ongoing matter and held this
provision “merely requires the agency to provide a specific reason for
withholding the records. KRS 17.150, therefore, makes the records at issue
exempt from disclosure until there is no prospective law enforcement
action, so long as the agency specifies what that action is or could be.”
19-ORD-166 (citing 14-ORD-154); *see also City of Ft. Thomas v. Cincinnati
Enquirer*, 506 S.W.3d 842 (Ky. 2013)); 20-ORD-105; 14-ORD-223 (holding that
“current status of a criminal prosecution could be a factor affecting the
threshold for a showing of harm to the agency under KRS 61.878(1)(h) as
interpreted in *City of Fort Thomas*” and noting the enforcement action was
“at a very early stage and the effect of public release of the relevant
jail records upon the fairness of a future trial is” unpredictable);
16-ORD-162. Based upon the foregoing, KSP has justified its partial denial
of your     request, pursuant to KRS 17.150(2), incorporated into the Open
Records Act by KRS 61.878(1)(l).



However, KSP is partially complying with your request by attaching hereto a
copy of the initial Kentucky Incident-Based Reporting System (“KYIBRS”)
Report concerning this investigation, excluding the narrative portion (you
may receive fewer pages than the number indicated on the report). This
portion of the initial KYIBRS Report is subject to disclosure regardless of
the status of the investigation, pursuant to 09-ORD-205. KSP has redacted
certain personal information from the attached records pursuant to KRS
61.878(1)(a), which exempts “information of a personal nature where the
public disclosure thereof would constitute a clearly unwarranted invasion
of personal privacy.” *Id.* Here, such personal information consists of
social security numbers, home addresses, personal telephone numbers,
driver’s license numbers, dates of birth, etc., the disclosure of which may
leave persons at risk for identity theft and which does not serve the
purpose of the Open Records Act. *See Zink v. Commonwealth*, 902 S.W.2d 825
(Ky. App. 1994); *Kentucky New Era v. City of Hopkinsville,* 415 S.W.3d 76
(Ky. 2013) (affirming the “categorical redaction” of such information
relating to private individuals).

21-ORD-034 reaffirms the Open Records Act does not require public agencies
to answer questions or provide information regarding your specific request.
If you wish to appeal this decision, you may do so by filing a complaint
with the Office of the Attorney General, Open Records/Open Meetings
Division, The Capitol, 700 Capital Avenue, Suite 118, Frankfort, KY 40601,
pursuant to KRS 61.880(2), or by filing an original action in Franklin
Circuit Court under KRS 61.882. If you first appeal to the Attorney General
but are dissatisfied with the Attorney General’s decision, you may further
appeal to Circuit Court pursuant to KRS 61.880(5).

   292|### Key New Patterns Identified
   293|- **Workers' compensation cases require a different workflow**: WC claims involve adjuster approval for treatment, TTD payments, AWW disputes, and DWC proceedings — all absent from auto accident PI cases. The PHASE_DAG may need WC-specific milestones.
   294|- **Case decline is a formal process**: When no BI/UM coverage exists (PIP-only), the case must be formally declined with attorney authorization, closing letter, and carrier notifications. This is a terminal phase transition not previously documented.
   295|- **Police records collection via ORR is complex**: Multiple ORRs are needed per case, different evidence types come from different systems (Axon for body cam, NextRequest for reports), and processing times vary wildly (days to months).
   296|- **Unreachable clients are distinct from frustrated clients**: Some clients are simply hard to reach (VM full, wrong number) rather than frustrated. This pattern most impacts intake and can contribute to case decline.
   297|- **Case manager handoffs create knowledge gaps**: When cases outlast their original case manager, the incoming staff may re-request known information, damaging client trust.
   298|- **SOL alerts run on closed cases**: Filevine's automated SOL countdown does not account for case status — alerts continue even after a case is declined.
   299|- **4+ year litigation cases exist**: Multi-defendant cases with uninsured drivers can extend far beyond the typical 2-3 year PI case lifecycle.

   303|- **Case decline criteria**: Need more declined cases to understand the full decision framework

   305|- **Medicare conditional payments**: One case had Medicare involvement but no lien/conditional payment data
   306|- **Witness location in litigation**: One case showed efforts to locate vehicle passengers but no outcome data

I have a thorough understanding of the existing wiki and the patterns from these 42 closing cases. Now let me compile the knowledge and update/create articles. Based on my analysis, here are the key patterns I found:

1. **Case decline/closing is the dominant pattern** - ~30+ of the 42 cases are closing via decline (not settlement disbursement)
2. **Multiple distinct closing triggers**: Client MIA, client at fault, no coverage (BI/UM), treatment unrelated to MVA, client terminated representation, case not viable
3. **Post-decline Conduent notification** is a specific pattern
4. **GDrive document archival** is a specific closing step
5. **"5 star review" task fires even on declined cases** - a process anti-pattern
6. **SOL countdown continues on closed cases** - confirmed across many cases
7. **Client fired firm** (jacoriana-lightsy, james-morris-iii) - distinct closing trigger
8. **Same-day decline** pattern (charles-kirk, skylar-hunter)
9. **Closing from litigation due to client non-cooperation** (curtis-mcguire - didn't show for depo, attorney filed MTW)
10. **Lien holder notification after decline is a required step** - Conduent, Carelon, PIP carriers all need to be notified

Let me now update and create the wiki articles:

- [[intake-and-onboarding-workflow]] — coverage verification is part of intake
- [[case-phase-transitions]] — decline is a terminal phase transition
- [[attorney-review-triggers]] — case decline requires attorney authorization
- [[uninsured-motorist-pip-only-cases]] — the coverage gap that triggers most declines
- [[unreachable-client-patterns]] — persistent unreachability is a distinct decline trigger
- [[client-frustration-escalation]] — client who fires the firm creates a closing event

### Batch 6 Evidence (42 closing cases)

- In 42 closing cases from this batch, the following decline/close triggers were documented:
  - **No BI/UM coverage (PIP-only)**: ~8 cases — the most common trigger. Standard pattern: paralegal identifies coverage gap, flags attorney, attorney authorizes decline, closing letter sent.
  - **Client MIA / unreachable**: ~7 cases — client cannot be reached after multiple attempts over weeks/months. Phase changed to "Client MIA" as an intermediate status before "Closing." In one case (DOI 2/2024), the paralegal logged "still no luck reaching out to client" over 4+ months before the attorney changed status to "Client MIA" and eventually to "Closing."
  - **Client at fault**: ~4 cases — police report or investigation reveals the firm's client caused the accident. In one case (DOI 12/2024), the paralegal noted "@attorney review for drop. client at fault" and the case was declined within 2 days.
  - **Treatment unrelated to MVA**: 1 case — paralegal reviewed all medical records and determined "none of her treatment is MVA related" despite the client being in an MVA. The attorney had previously flagged the case as "ready for demand" months earlier, creating a delay when the demand could not be drafted.
  - **Client terminated representation**: 2 cases — client sent written termination (email) stating "I no longer need your service" or "I'm no longer going to need you going any further." In both cases, the firm immediately changed status to Closing and sent the closing letter.
  - **Client non-cooperation in litigation**: 1 case — in a litigated case, the client failed to appear for their scheduled deposition despite attorney sending multiple texts, calling 5+ times the day before and morning of. The attorney filed a Motion to Withdraw. Defense counsel then filed a Motion to Dismiss for Failure to Prosecute with sanctions. The withdrawal letter was left at the client's door.
  - **Case not viable (medical malpractice inquiry)**: 1 case — client wanted to sue a hospital for treatment quality, not pursue the original injury claim. Attorney directed "decline and close."
  - **Client already had attorney**: 1 case — client signed intake documents in error while already represented by another firm. Declined same day.
  - **Returning client at fault**: 1 case — returning client (previous case with the firm) was at fault in the new accident. Signed at intake but closed at Lead stage when fault confirmed.

- **Closing Sequence Observed Across All 42 Cases**:
  1. Phase change to "Closing" (logged with staff name, old status, new status)
  2. Automated task fires: "Send Closing letter to Client. Be sure to send copies to BI, PIP, & all Lien Holders."
  3. Decline representation letter mailed/emailed to client
  4. Notification sent to all carriers (BI, PIP) and lien holders (Conduent, Carelon, etc.)
  5. Documents moved to permanent storage (GDrive): "All documents moved to GDrive" logged
  6. Automated "5 star review" task fires (even on declined cases — anti-pattern)

- **Post-Decline Carrier Notification**: In 3+ cases, Conduent (Medicaid subrogation) or PIP carriers contacted the firm months after decline asking about the case. The firm responded: "We no longer represent this client" and requested removal of PIP reservations. In one case, the firm emailed Conduent directly: "Our office has formally declined the case... We no longer represent her for the [date] date of accident." This confirms that lien holders must be proactively notified at close.

- **Carelon Lien Closure**: In one case (DOI 2/2024), Carelon (Anthem subrogation) confirmed "File closed 0 paid" — confirming that lien holders close their files when notified of case decline. The firm then requested written confirmation of the zero-lien status, which took 2+ months of follow-up to obtain.

- **Same-Day Decline Pattern**: In 3 cases, the firm signed the client at intake and declined the same day or within 1-2 days. Triggers: client at fault (confirmed from police report at intake), no viable claim, client already had attorney.

- **Closing from Any Phase**: Closings were observed from the following prior statuses: Lead, File Setup, Treatment, Demand in Progress, Negotiation, Litigation, and Client MIA. The most common source was File Setup (10+ cases), followed by Treatment (5+ cases). Closing from Litigation (via attorney withdrawal) was the least common but most complex.

- **SOL Countdown Continues After Closing**: In 15+ cases, SOL countdown alerts continued firing for months/years after the case was moved to "Closing" status. The automated Filevine SOL system does not account for case status, creating noise in the task queue.

- **GDrive Archival**: In 2 cases, the explicit log entry "All documents moved to GDrive" confirmed the file archival step. This was logged separately from the closing letter, suggesting it's a manual step performed by staff after the closing letter is sent."}

title: Client-Initiated Termination of Representation
slug: client-initiated-termination

# Client-Initiated Termination of Representation

> In some cases, the client fires the firm rather than the firm declining the case. This can happen at any phase — from early file setup through active litigation. The termination is typically communicated via email or text, and the firm must immediately change the case status to Closing, send the standard closing letter, and notify all carriers and lien holders. This pattern is distinct from firm-initiated decline (coverage gaps, client MIA) because the client is an active participant who has decided to leave.

## Pattern

### Termination Triggers
1. **Client dissatisfaction with progress**: Client perceives the case is not moving forward
2. **Client found another attorney**: Client has already retained new counsel
3. **Client no longer wants to pursue**: Client decides the case is not worth continuing
4. **Client disagrees with case strategy**: Client wants to pursue a different type of claim than what the firm is willing to handle

### Termination Communication
- Clients typically terminate via email, using language like:
  - "I am writing to officially notify you that I am terminating your services immediately"
  - "I'm no longer going to need you going any further"
  - "I appreciate your help... give me a call to discuss further action"
- Some terminations are polite and forward-looking; others are curt and immediate
- Guardian/parent may terminate on behalf of a minor client

### Firm Response
1. Immediately change case status to "Closing"
2. Standard closing task fires: "Send Closing letter to Client"
3. Notify all carriers (BI, PIP) and lien holders
4. Do NOT contest the termination — comply immediately
5. If in litigation, file a Motion to Withdraw with the court

## Evidence

- In a minor's MVA case (DOI 11/2024), the guardian (mother) emailed the firm: "I am writing you today for my minor behalf... to let you know I no longer need your service. I am writing to officially notify you that I am terminating your services immediately. You no longer represent me for this accident injury claim." The firm changed status to Closing the next business day and sent closing letters.
- In a WC/MVA case (DOI 8/2025), the client emailed: "This is [Client]. I'm no longer going to need you going any further. I appreciate your help in getting me represented and looking forward to working with you in the future if need be." The firm changed status to Closing the same day. Notably, this client had only been with the firm for 6 days (intake 8/6, termination 8/8).
- In a litigated MVA case (DOI 12/2023), the client became uncooperative during litigation — failing to appear for his scheduled deposition despite the attorney texting and calling 5+ times the day before and morning of. The attorney filed a Motion to Withdraw with the court. Defense counsel then filed a Motion to Dismiss for Failure to Prosecute and sought sanctions against the client directly, noting "we do not think counsel did anything wrong." The withdrawal letter was physically left at the client's door. This represents an involuntary termination driven by client non-cooperation rather than explicit client request.
- In a settled MVA case (DOI 1/2024), the client already had an attorney (another firm handling her case). She signed intake documents in error. The firm declined same-day upon discovering the existing representation.

## Agent Guidance

1. When a client sends a termination notice, do NOT try to talk them out of it — acknowledge and process immediately
2. Change case status to "Closing" the same day the termination is received
3. Follow the complete closing checklist: closing letter to client, copies to all carriers and lien holders
4. If the case is in litigation, immediately prepare a Motion to Withdraw and serve on all parties
5. Preserve all case documents — the client or their new attorney may request the file
6. Log the termination communication in the activity log with the exact date and method
7. Do NOT continue working the case after receiving the termination notice

## Pitfalls

- Delaying the closing process after receiving termination (creates liability)
- Not notifying carriers and lien holders promptly (they may continue directing communications to the firm)
- Not filing a Motion to Withdraw in litigated cases (leaves the firm as counsel of record)
- Assuming the client will change their mind and not processing the termination
- Not preserving the termination communication in the file (needed for potential malpractice defense)

## Related

- [[case-decline-and-closing-workflow]] — the closing process that follows termination
- [[client-frustration-escalation]] — frustration often precedes client termination
- [[case-phase-transitions]] — termination is a terminal phase transition from any phase
"}

# Closing Phase Triggers Inappropriate Review Request

> When a case moves to "Closing" status in Filevine, an automated task fires: "Do you think client will give 5 star review? If so reach out to client and send link." This task fires indiscriminately — including on cases where the client was at fault, the client fired the firm, the case was declined due to no coverage, or the client was unreachable for months. Requesting a review from these clients would be inappropriate and potentially damaging to the firm's reputation.

## How the Concepts Interact

### The Causal Chain
1. **[[case-decline-and-closing-workflow]]**: Case is moved to "Closing" status for any reason
2. **Filevine Automation**: Phase change to "Closing" triggers automated task queue
3. **Review Request Task**: "Do you think client will give 5 star review?" fires regardless of close reason
4. **Staff Confusion**: Paralegal must manually evaluate whether the task is appropriate

## Evidence

- In 10+ closing cases from this batch, the "Do you think client will give 5 star review?" task was logged on the same day or within days of the phase change to Closing.
- Specific inappropriate examples observed:
  - A case where the client terminated representation after 6 days
  - A case where the client was at fault and the firm declined
  - A case where the client had never been successfully contacted (filed at intake, never reached)
  - A case where the firm filed a Motion to Withdraw after the client failed to appear for a deposition
  - A case where the client signed intake documents in error (already had another attorney)

## Agent Guidance

1. The "5 star review" task should only be sent to clients who had a positive outcome (settlement disbursed, case resolved favorably)
2. When this task fires on a declined/terminated case, dismiss it immediately without action
3. Consider proposing a Filevine automation update to suppress review requests when the prior status was anything other than "Settlement" or "Negotiation" (indicating a resolved case)
4. Never send a review request to a client whose case was declined or who fired the firm

## Related

- [[case-decline-and-closing-workflow]] — the closing workflow that triggers this anti-pattern
- [[case-phase-transitions]] — the phase change that fires the automated task
"}

## Related", "new_string": "- Not keeping disbursement records accessible after case closure

### Batch 6 Evidence (3 post-close carrier contacts)

- In 3 closing cases from this batch, carriers or lien holders contacted the firm months after the case was declined/closed:
  - In an MVA case (DOI 9/2023), the PIP carrier (Progressive) sent "numerous emails and letters" to the firm about pending bills months after the case was declined and the firm no longer represented the client. The carrier escalated: "I have been unable to get a response." The firm finally responded confirming they no longer represented the client and asked the carrier to "remove any remaining PIP reservations and process any remaining bills."
  - In an MVA case (DOI 1/2024), a Conduent Medicaid subrogation analyst sent a follow-up request months after decline. The firm responded: "Our office has formally declined the case... We no longer represent her."
  - In an MVA case (DOI 2/2024), Carelon (Anthem subrogation) confirmed "File closed 0 paid" — but the firm had to follow up for 2+ months to obtain written confirmation of the zero-lien status.
- These post-close contacts confirm that lien holders and carriers continue to track cases independently of the firm's status. Proactive notification at closing is essential to prevent extended back-and-forth.

## Related"}

# Attorney Withdrawal from Active Litigation

> When a case is in active litigation and the attorney-client relationship breaks down, the attorney cannot simply close the file — they must file a Motion to Withdraw with the court. This is a more complex closing process than pre-litigation decline because the firm is counsel of record. Client non-cooperation (especially failure to appear for depositions) is the primary trigger observed. Defense counsel may simultaneously file motions for sanctions and dismissal.

## Pattern

### Withdrawal Triggers in Litigation
1. **Client fails to appear for deposition**: Despite multiple reminders (texts, calls, in-person delivery), the client does not show up
2. **Client becomes unreachable during litigation**: Cannot be contacted to coordinate required appearances
3. **Client explicitly terminates**: Client fires the firm during active litigation

### Withdrawal Process
1. **Attorney decision**: Attorney determines the relationship is no longer viable
2. **Motion to Withdraw filed**: Formal motion filed with the circuit court
3. **Service on client**: Withdrawal letter served on the client (may require physical delivery to client's address)
4. **Court hearing**: Motion set for hearing at motion hour (typically 2-3 weeks out)
5. **Defense response**: Defense counsel may or may not object; may simultaneously file their own motions
6. **Order entered**: Court grants withdrawal; case proceeds with client pro se or is dismissed

### Defense Counsel Response
- Defense counsel may file a Motion to Dismiss for Failure to Prosecute
- Defense counsel may seek sanctions (costs for wasted deposition, travel)
- Defense counsel may note explicitly that "we do not think counsel did anything wrong" — directing sanctions at the client rather than the attorney
- Defense counsel typically does not object to the withdrawal itself

## Evidence

- In a litigated MVA case (DOI 12/2023, KY, Union Circuit Court), the client failed to appear for his scheduled deposition at the Union County Public Library. The attorney had:
  - Called the client 3 times the day before the deposition
  - Texted: "If you fail to appear for your deposition in the morning it will have a negative impact on your case... If you are unable to obtain transportation, please let me know, I will be in Union County around 8:30 in the morning"
  - Called 4+ times the morning of the deposition
  - Texted: "We are at the library for your deposition. Please advise if you need transportation"
  - The client did not respond or appear
  - The attorney filed a Motion to Withdraw with the court the next day, set for motion hour on 7/14/25
  - Defense counsel responded: "We will be filing a Motion to Dismiss for Failure to Prosecute and for costs related to the deposition. We will be seeking this sanction against Plaintiff directly and noting that we do not think counsel did anything wrong."
  - The withdrawal letter was physically "left at client door"
  - The case was changed from Litigation to Closing status
  - This case had already involved extensive litigation effort: prior discovery, defense counsel correspondence, and the attorney traveling to a rural county (Union County) for the deposition

## Agent Guidance

1. When a client fails to appear for a deposition, immediately notify the attorney
2. Document ALL outreach attempts (calls, texts, physical delivery) with timestamps — these become evidence supporting the Motion to Withdraw
3. If the attorney decides to withdraw, prepare the Motion to Withdraw for filing
4. Serve the withdrawal letter on the client via the most reliable method available (mail, email, AND physical delivery)
5. Notify defense counsel of the Motion to Withdraw and provide a copy
6. Continue tracking the case until the court grants the Motion — the firm remains counsel of record until the order is entered
7. After withdrawal is granted, follow the standard closing checklist (notify carriers, lien holders, archive documents)

## Pitfalls

- Not documenting outreach attempts before the deposition (weakens the Motion to Withdraw)
- Not filing the Motion to Withdraw promptly after the breakdown (leaves the firm exposed to defense motions)
- Not serving the client with the withdrawal letter (procedural deficiency)
- Assuming the case is closed before the court grants the Motion (firm is still counsel of record)
- Not notifying defense counsel of the withdrawal promptly

## Related

- [[case-decline-and-closing-workflow]] — the closing process after withdrawal is granted
- [[client-initiated-termination]] — when the client fires the firm during litigation
- [[litigation-process-patterns]] — the litigation context in which withdrawal occurs
- [[case-phase-transitions]] — Litigation -> Closing is the phase transition
"}

## 2026-04-12 — Batch 6 Compilation (42 CLOSING cases)

**Compiler**: Wiki Compiler Agent (subagent)
**Cases Processed**: 42 (all CLOSING cases from batch4_2_cases.txt)
**Source Data**:
- 42 cases with activity logs ranging from 2-104 entries each
- All cases in "Closing" status — representing the complete case closure lifecycle
- Mix of decline types: no coverage (~8), client MIA (~7), client at fault (~4), client terminated (~2), treatment unrelated (~1), litigation withdrawal (~1), not viable (~3), miscellaneous (~16)

### Articles Created: 3 (2 concepts, 1 connection)

1. `client-initiated-termination` — New concept: client fires the firm (via email/text), distinct from firm-initiated decline. Termination triggers, communication patterns, immediate processing required. Evidence from 4 cases including a guardian terminating for a minor and a client who terminated after 6 days.

2. `attorney-withdrawal-in-litigation` — New concept: when a litigated case must be closed via Motion to Withdraw. Triggered by client non-cooperation (deposition no-show). Defense counsel response pattern (sanctions against client, no objection to withdrawal). Evidence from 1 detailed case with extensive documentation.

3. `closing-triggers-review-request-antipattern` (connection) — New connection: Filevine automation fires "5 star review" request on ALL closings including declined/terminated cases. Anti-pattern observed in 10+ cases. Guidance to suppress review requests on non-settlement closings.

### Articles Updated: 8

1. `case-decline-and-closing-workflow` — evidence_count: 9->42, confidence: medium->high. Major update with 42 closing cases documenting: 9 distinct closing triggers, standardized closing sequence (phase change -> closing letter -> carrier notification -> GDrive archival), "Client MIA" intermediate status pattern, same-day decline pattern, closing from any phase (Lead through Litigation), SOL countdown continuing on closed cases, post-decline Conduent/Carelon notification requirements.

2. `unreachable-client-patterns` — evidence_count: 78->85. Added 7 cases where unreachable clients directly caused case decline. "Client MIA" intermediate Filevine status confirmed across 5+ cases. Longest unreachability before close: 17 months.

3. `case-phase-transitions` — evidence_count: 162->204. Added 42 closing phase transitions from various source statuses (Lead, File Setup, Treatment, Demand in Progress, Negotiation, Litigation, Client MIA).

4. `post-settlement-provider-disputes` — evidence_count: 5->8. Added 3 cases where carriers/lien holders contacted the firm months after case decline, not just settlement.

5. `statute-of-limitations-monitoring` — evidence_count: 124->139. Confirmed SOL countdown continuing on 15+ closed cases (Filevine automation does not suppress SOL alerts based on case status).

6. `client-frustration-escalation` — evidence_count: 86->90. Added evidence of frustration leading to client termination (2 cases) and client non-cooperation as extreme frustration endpoint.

7. `pip-claims-management` — evidence_count: 137->142. Added post-decline PIP carrier notification patterns.

8. `medicaid-lien-management` — evidence_count: 34->37. Added post-decline Conduent notification patterns.

Also incremented: `lien-management-health-insurance` (87->90), `file-transfer-from-prior-counsel` (5->6).

### Key Patterns Identified from CLOSING Cases

- **Case decline is far more common than settlement**: Of 42 closing cases, the vast majority were declines rather than post-settlement closings. The firm signs many cases that are ultimately not viable.
- **9 distinct closing triggers identified**: No coverage, client MIA, client at fault, treatment unrelated, client terminated, litigation withdrawal, case not viable, client already represented, returning client at fault.
- **"Client MIA" is a standard intermediate status**: Filevine has a "Client MIA" status used as a holding pattern between active case management and formal closing. Cases can remain in this status for months.
- **Same-day decline is a real pattern**: 3 cases were opened and declined on the same day (or within 1-2 days) when viability issues were immediately apparent at intake.
- **Closing from Litigation is the most complex close type**: Requires Motion to Withdraw, court approval, and coordination with defense counsel.
- **Post-decline carrier contacts create ongoing work**: Conduent, Carelon, and PIP carriers continue to track cases independently and will contact the firm months after decline.
- **"5 star review" automation fires inappropriately on declines**: A process anti-pattern where the review request task fires on all closings regardless of outcome type.
- **GDrive archival is a manual step**: Documents are moved to GDrive as a separate manual task after the closing letter is sent.
- **SOL alerts are noisy after closure**: The Filevine SOL countdown system does not account for case status, generating alerts on closed cases for months/years.
- **Defense counsel generally cooperates with withdrawal**: In the one observed litigation withdrawal, defense counsel did not object and directed sanctions at the client rather than the attorney.

### Confidence Upgrades
- `case-decline-and-closing-workflow`: medium -> high (42 cases)

### Gaps Identified
- **Post-settlement closing**: These 42 cases were primarily declines; the wiki still needs more post-settlement closing data (disbursement complete -> archive workflow)
- **Client review/satisfaction at close**: Only the inappropriate "5 star review" automation was observed; no actual client satisfaction data
- **Case reopening after close**: No cases showed reopening; unknown whether/how a closed case can be reopened
- **Malpractice exposure on close**: No data on whether declined clients pursue malpractice claims against the firm"}

---

SUMMARY OF COMPILATION

WHAT I DID:
- Read the full AGENTS.md schema and all 57 existing concept articles + 22 connection articles
- Read the case list (42 cases in /tmp/batch4_2_cases.txt)
- Read activity logs for all 42 cases (sampling first 10 + last 10 for cases with >50 logs; reading all logs for smaller cases)
- Identified closing-specific patterns across all cases
- Updated 10 existing articles with new evidence and incremented evidence counts
- Created 2 new concept articles and 1 new connection article
- Appended a detailed compilation summary to wiki/log.md
- Did NOT rewrite wiki/index.md

WHAT I FOUND:
These 42 cases were overwhelmingly DECLINE closings (not post-settlement closings). Key discoveries:

1. 9 distinct closing triggers: no coverage, client MIA, client at fault, treatment unrelated to MVA, client terminated representation, litigation withdrawal (depo no-show), case not viable, client already represented, returning client at fault

2. "Client MIA" is a standard Filevine intermediate status before formal closing, used in 5+ cases

3. Same-day decline pattern exists (3 cases opened and declined within 24 hours)

4. Attorney withdrawal from litigation due to client non-cooperation (deposition no-show) is the most complex close type -- requires Motion to Withdraw, defense counsel files sanctions

5. "5 star review" automation anti-pattern: Filevine fires review request task on ALL closings including declined/terminated cases

6. Post-decline carrier contacts persist for months (Conduent, Carelon, PIP carriers)

7. SOL countdown continues indefinitely on closed cases (Filevine automation gap)

8. GDrive archival is a manual closing step
